## 2026-07-12 — D-324: ISSUE — the SonarCloud scan crashes at `opendir '<workspace>/C:/proj/context'` (exit 3); confirmed server-side/plugin, NOT repo-fixable (Task 224)

**The symptom:** every SonarCloud run since 2026-07-11 ~12:42 (last clean 10:07) fails in the JS/Web analyzer (`WebSensor` → `BridgeServerImpl.analyzeProject`, plugin v13.2.0) with `ENOENT ... opendir '/home/runner/.../C:/proj/context'` — a Windows-shaped path resolved relative to the Linux CI workspace, exit 3.

**Proven NOT in the repo (grepped exhaustively):** the string `C:/proj/context` is in no tracked file — not `sonar-project.properties`, no `packages/*/src/*.mjs`, not the `tests/` path-literals (`C:/proj/new.txt`, `/proj/context/DECISIONS.md` are close but not it), not the generated `coverage/lcov.info` (SF: paths clean), no tsconfig/jsconfig, no HTML, no package.json path field.

**Three repo-side fixes TRIED, all failed identically** (the diagnostic that proves it's server-side): (1) `sonar.exclusions += context/**, docs/**` → crash recurred; (2) `+= tests/**` → crash recurred; (3) `sonar.projectBaseDir=.` → crash recurred. Because the crash is a PROJECT-level opendir (before per-file analysis), file-scoped config can't reach it.

**Conclusion:** the path is a STALE SERVER-SIDE project setting (an old Windows dev `sonar.sources`/path set once in the web UI) OR a v13.2.0 plugin defect — reachable only from the SonarCloud web UI / SonarSource support, not this repo. The `sonarcloud.yml` header already warns CI-analysis is mutually-exclusive with server-side "Automatic Analysis"; a re-enabled Automatic Analysis running stale stored config is the leading hypothesis. **The fix (Task 224, needs the maintainer's web-UI access):** clear the stale source path / confirm Automatic Analysis is OFF / else a support ticket.

**NOT a blocker:** SonarCloud is advisory — CI + CodeQL + gitleaks + osv-scanner + npm-audit gate real quality, all green; v0.5.1 published successfully through this red. The `context/docs/tests` exclusions were KEPT (correct hygiene — a code scanner shouldn't analyze non-source prose regardless); the `projectBaseDir` line was reverted (didn't help, added noise). Distinct from D-39 (the OTHER standing SonarCloud known-red — hotspots-reviewed, a different accepted-red). _Relates D-39, the `sonarcloud.yml` + `sonar-project.properties`, Task 224, D-324._

## 2026-07-12 — D-323: FIX — Task 222: the Task-205 MCP-preflight INFORMS but no longer PROMPTS on a plain `cmk install` (the hazard is upgrade-only)

**The catch (the user, live in the v0.5.1 cut-gate):** a plain `cmk install --with-semantic` into a throwaway project printed the Task-205 running-MCP-server heads-up + the interactive `[y/N]` stop-offer — while this dev repo's OWN `cmk mcp serve` + `cmk-auto-extract` children were live. The user: *"if we don't care and type N then why do users need this?"* → then the sharper mechanism: *"we are installing this on another folder but it is install on this folder and the kit is live here"* — i.e. the shared GLOBAL binary is served by this repo's live processes, and the prompt fired on a project install that can't touch those locked DLLs.

**The diagnosis (primary-source, not assumption — the user forced the check):** `warnRunningMcpServers` was wired at the top of `runInstall` with an interactive default-NO stop-offer (Task 205 sub-item (a), shipped D-314). But the DLL-lock half-install it warns about (D-302) can ONLY occur on `npm install -g` (the global upgrade) — a command the kit can't hook. A project `cmk install` never touches the global `node_modules`, so the stop's answer is ALWAYS N: the prompt was correct INFORMATION attached to the wrong INTERACTION. Confirmed behaving-to-spec (not a regression) — best-effort + never-throw + default-N mean nothing broke either way; this is a UX refinement of a deliberate choice.

**The fix (two passes — the first didn't go far enough):** `warnRunningMcpServers` gains an `offerStop` option (default false). With `offerStop: true`, the full interactive stop-offer is restored, reserved for a future upgrade context (a `cmk update` / a doctor path) that IS the moment to act. `runInstall`'s two call sites pass no `offerStop`. The interactivity gate + the D-315 hermeticity guard are untouched (orthogonal to the ask), so a non-TTY still never scans.

**AMENDMENT (same session, the user's second catch — "why do I need all this bullshit?"):** the first pass made the plain-install path INFORM-not-prompt, but kept PRINTING the multi-line note (header + per-PID list + the two DLL-lock caveat lines + the reassurance line) — 5+ lines of noise on every install for a hazard that (a) a project install can't cause and (b) isn't even actionable there. The user was right: that's still bullshit. **The plain-install path is now FULLY SILENT** — `if (!offerStop) return;` fires BEFORE any emit. Nothing prints, nothing asks. The hazard's real safety net is unchanged and sufficient: if an `npm install -g` upgrade ever DOES half-break on a locked DLL, the bin-boundary recovery message (Task 205's OTHER half, D-314) prints the exact 2-step fix at the moment it matters. So: silence on install + recovery-on-actual-breakage, with the verbose warning + stop-offer reserved entirely for an `offerStop` caller. Live-verified: `cmk install` with 2 servers running prints ONLY the install result (no PID list, no lecture). The meta-lesson: an over-eager INTERACTION and an over-eager OUTPUT are two separate wants — fixing the prompt didn't fix the noise; the user had to catch it twice.

**Verified:** the preflight suite (22 tests, incl. 2 new: "prints the note but NEVER asks" + "offerStop restores the stop-offer" + the 2 pre-existing ask-tests updated to opt in). Full suite 2922 green. Live: rebuilt the tarball, reinstalled the global, ran `cmk install` into a fresh project with 2 servers live → completed with NO prompt (the note-print path is non-TTY-gated, so the deterministic note test covers it; the real-TTY note is the operator's confirmation). NOT a v0.5.1 blocker — filed + fixed as a fast-follow because the context was live; if it hadn't been mid-gate it would have been a normal v0.5.x task. _Relates Task 205 / D-302 / D-314 (`warnRunningMcpServers`, `mcp-procs.mjs`), the v0.5.1 cut-gate (the surfacing run), Task 222, D-323._

## 2026-07-12 — D-322: FIX — Task 220: duplicate managed blocks FOLD on install, remove-all on uninstall, HC-9 flags them

**The bug (the 2026-07-10 review's install findings 1/2, D-312; live-reproduced by the reviewer):** `claude-md.mjs` and `install-agent.mjs` both matched the managed block NON-globally, so a file carrying TWO `claude-memory-kit:start/:end` blocks (a manual copy-paste, a kept-both-sides merge resolution, a prior double-append) had only its FIRST block refreshed on reinstall — orphaning a permanently-stale duplicate every future install ignored — and uninstall left the second block behind, contradicting the byte-preserve-outside-markers / clean-removal contract.

**The fix (fold, not just flag — the D-169 self-healing posture):** a `findAllManagedBlocks` scanner pairs every start marker with the first end marker after it (an end-less start extends to EOF — the same orphan recovery as before, necessarily last). `injectClaudeMdBlock` folds ALL blocks into the single refreshed one at the first block's position, preserving user bytes between/around duplicates in order; version comparison runs against the NEWEST version across blocks so a stale duplicate cannot let an older kit through the downgrade gate ('unchanged' short-circuits only for a byte-identical SINGLE block — duplicates always fold). `removeClaudeMdBlock` strips every block (back-to-front, per-block whitespace cleanup identical to the old single-block semantics). `install-agent.mjs` gets the same treatment via a global regex (first match → fresh block, rest → removed + blank-run collapse; uninstall removes all). `findManagedBlock` now exposes `duplicateCount`, and HC-9 FAILs on a duplicate — before the version compare, since a duplicate is an integrity fault regardless of versions — with `cmk install` as the recovery (which now actually fixes it).

**One deliberate behavior refinement:** a stray END marker BEFORE the first start used to make the single-block finder treat the block as corrupted-to-EOF; the scanner now pairs each start with the next end AFTER it, so the stray leading end is ignored as user noise and the real block parses normally — strictly better recovery, no test pinned the old shape.

**Two-pass review record:** self-review caught the idempotency breaker mid-flight (an unconditional blank-run collapse on the instruction-file refresh path would have mutated user bytes + broken the unchanged check on EVERY single-block install — now fold-path-only, with a byte-idempotency regression test). The skill pass then confirmed the core algorithm clean under adversarial traces and surfaced ONE real fix: the downgrade-blocked path skipped the fold, so HC-9's cmk-install advice would LOOP in the exact merge-imported-newer-duplicate scenario the task heals — duplicates now fold to the NEWEST existing block's content even while the version change stays refused. Documented-accepts: the fold-path blank-run collapse touches the whole instruction file (matches the uninstall precedent); the claude-md fold leaves a slightly wider blank seam (stable — next run hits the single-block unchanged path); install-agent's orphan-start blindness is pre-existing and out of scope.

**Verified:** two-block fixture tests across all three surfaces (cli-claude-md: fold + remove-all + duplicateCount; cli-install-agent: steering-file fold + remove-all with user content preserved; cli-version-drift: HC-9 duplicate fail even at matching versions). Full suite 2917 green. Stress skipped with reason: pure file-mutation logic, no spawn/hook/concurrency surface (the Task-214 class). _Relates D-312 (the review), design §13.1 (the loader-block contract, updated), HC-9 (HEALTH-CHECKS.md row updated), Task 220, D-322._

## 2026-07-11 — D-321: FIX + NOTE — Task 219: `busy_timeout` shipped as a CONTRACT PIN — the audit premise was wrong (better-sqlite3 already defaulted to 5000ms)

**The premise correction (the load-bearing part):** Task 219 / design §16.34 / the 2026-07-10 recall review (D-312 finding 2) all assumed `openIndexDb` inherits SQLite's raw 0ms busy-timeout — "two writers contending throw SQLITE_BUSY immediately." **Wrong for our driver:** better-sqlite3's `Database` constructor defaults its `timeout` option to **5000ms** (`lib/database.js`: `const timeout = 'timeout' in options ? options.timeout : 5000` — the primary source), and `openIndexDb` is the kit's only real DB-open site. The TDD red-first run caught it empirically: the "RED" cross-process test — a second process holding `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` while the parent writes — PASSED with zero code change (the parent waited ~600ms and landed). The protection existed; it was accidental, not contractual.

**What shipped:** `db.pragma('busy_timeout = 5000')` in `openIndexDb` (explicit contract — survives a future driver major changing the default or a `timeout: 0` option) + the §16.35 REAL concurrent-writer test that was deferred since 2026-05-27 (`tests/fixtures/hold-index-write-lock.mjs` child acquires the write lock, sentinel-synchronized; the parent's write waits out the hold, with timing assertions proving actual contention). §16.34 + §16.35 marked shipped with the original analyses preserved per the decision-trail rule. NO behavior change → no CHANGELOG entry (the honest-docs rule: don't announce a fix for a failure that couldn't occur).

**The meta-lesson (the "did you check?" family, applied to an AUDIT):** a security/reliability review's finding is itself a CLAIM subject to primary-source verification — the 2026-07-10 review reasoned from SQLite's default, not the driver's. The red-first discipline is what surfaced it: a "fix" whose RED test passes before the fix is a wrong premise, not a bug. _Relates D-312 (the review), design §16.34/§16.35, Task 219, D-321._

## 2026-07-11 — D-320: FIX + DECISION — Task 216: the Poison_Guard side doors are closed (one shared `screenBeforeCommittedWrite`); transcripts screen SECRETS-ONLY and WITHHOLD; trust increases re-screen

**The fix (the 2026-07-10 security review's findings 3/4/5/6, D-312):** `checkPoisonGuard` was one well-built chokepoint on the DIRECT write path with structurally-identical unscreened side doors around it. One shared helper — `screenBeforeCommittedWrite(text, {projectRoot, source, ts, scope})` (screens + best-effort-logs redacted; the CALLER decides rejection semantics) — is now called at every side door. Full site table: design §6.7.1. Per-site shapes: curate/distill screen **input pre-Haiku** (a poisoned SOURCE reproduces in the summary every retry — pre-call screening turns the retry loop from a nightly/weekly Haiku bill into a regex pass; skill-review finding 2) **and output post-Haiku**; distill's assembled `recent.md` has a backstop for LEGACY artifacts plus an **empty-assembly clobber guard** (a poisoned-only/all-empty run must never truncate a good `recent.md` and stamp a fresh mtime that MASKS HC-10 — skill-review finding 1, CONFIRMED); the persona-review queue drops the entry with a REDACTED user-tier audit entry (new `POISON_GUARD_REJECTED` reason code — no project-scoped log exists at a user-tier site).

**DECISION — transcripts WITHHOLD (not defer) and screen SECRETS-ONLY:** a Poison_Guard hit is a PERMANENT condition, unlike a judge failure — deferring would re-judge (re-bill) the same batch every run and starve the 2 `PROMOTE_MAX_FILES_PER_RUN` slots oldest-first (the D-298 starvation class: two poisoned old dates would block promotion entirely). A withheld batch leaves a content-free, offset-stamped marker in the committed file (idempotent across crash-replay by matching the batch-START offset prefix), advances the watermark, and keeps the raw text in the gitignored live buffer. Scope is `secrets` because a committed transcript is a verbatim RECORD never injected into context (search-last-resort; the read-side defense is Task 70's inject-time re-scan) — the full catalog's injection patterns would routinely withhold transcripts of any repo that DISCUSSES prompt injection, i.e. THIS dogfood repo, daily (skill-review finding 3). All other sites screen the FULL catalog: `recent.md`/`archive.md`/queues CAN reach injected context.

**DECISION — trust increases re-screen; `lessonsPromote` needs no gate:** `overrideTrust` pre-scans every matched location (fact body + scratchpad bullet via a read-only peek) BEFORE any mutation and rejects the increase (`category: poison_guard`, nothing mutated) if the new level ranks above a location's current trust and its text trips the CURRENT catalog — content screened under an older catalog must re-pass before being blessed upward; decreases stay allowed (lowering trust on poisoned content must remain possible). `lessonsPromote` routes through `promoteCandidatesToUserTier` → `memoryWrite`, which already re-screens on the new write.

**Documented-accepts (skill-review findings 7/8):** a RECURRING poisoned persona candidate re-audits once per weekly pass (a durable seen-set would be an ADR-0002 sidecar for ≤1 NDJSON line/week); the gate-scan→mutate TOCTOU is the kit's standing single-writer posture. **Deferred with trigger:** a doctor HC surfacing "same poison-guard skip ≥2 consecutive curate/distill log entries → name the offending source file" — trigger: a repeated poison-guard skip observed in a real corpus (the finding-2 residual: the skip is currently NDJSON-only).

**Two-pass review record:** self-review caught the defer-forever starvation + the silent persona drop pre-skill-review; the skill pass then surfaced 9 findings (1 CONFIRMED Important: the empty-assembly clobber; the rest as above) — all fixed inline or documented-accept. 15-test suite (`tests/cli-screen-committed-writes.test.js`), incl. Door-3 zero-response backends proving the pre-screens skip the model call. _Relates D-312 (the review + the two already-shipped halves), D-298 (the starvation class the withhold avoids), ADR-0002 (why no seen-set sidecar), Task 70 (the read-side re-scan), Task 216, D-320._

## 2026-07-11 — D-319: FIX — Task 215: the nightly distill runs WINDOWLESS (a VBS `wscript` shim); the real-machine probe chose the mechanism

**The bug (D-311, the user's live observation):** the 23:00 `cmk-daily-distill` schtask launches `node.exe` directly in the interactive session, so Windows opens a visible console window over the user's screen for the run's duration — a black box popping up nightly, contradicting "invisible memory that just works." NOT the Task-81 spawn-flag class (`windowsHide` on children the KIT spawns) — this is the window Task Scheduler itself opens for the scheduled console binary, a registration-shape problem.

**The mechanism, chosen by REAL-machine probes (not docs — the task's own requirement):**
- Inspected the actual registered task: `LogonType=Interactive, Hidden=False` — the visible-window cause confirmed.
- REJECTED (a) S4U / run-whether-logged-on (session-0, no window): `Set-ScheduledTask … -LogonType S4U` returned **"Access is denied"** without elevation — it needs SeBatchLogonRight/admin, i.e. a UAC prompt we won't force on a non-admin install.
- CHOSE (b) the windowless launcher: `register-crons` writes a VBS shim into gitignored `context/.locks/<entry>-run.vbs` and registers `/TR` as `wscript.exe //B //Nologo "<shim>"`. The shim runs the real command via `WshShell.Run(cmd, 0, True)` — windowStyle 0 (hidden), True (wait for exit, so the schtask's LastResult still reflects the real run). PROVEN on the real machine: the generated shim, invoked exactly as `/TR` would, executed a real command with **zero window** and completed.

**Robustness:** best-effort — a shim-write failure (no projectRoot, read-only dir) falls back to the direct visible command rather than failing registration. 5 unit tests (shim content / argv-with-shim / shim-write via the writeFile seam / fallback-on-failure / the buildWindowlessShim escaping). **Honestly owed:** the ONE step autopilot can't take — registering + firing a REAL scheduled trigger on the user's machine — is flagged in the task as the user's manual live-verify (the mechanism is probe-proven; the end-to-end schtask run is theirs). _Relates D-311 (the observation), Task 203 (WakeToRun, same registration surface — batched), Task 81 (the spawned-children sibling), the CLAUDE.md live-test discipline (the probe that chose the mechanism), D-319._

## 2026-07-11 — D-318: FIX — Task 214: the name-guard now scans UNTRACKED files, closing the exact D-310 hole (a screen that lied to the human relying on it)

**The incident (D-310, THIS session):** a research note carried the maintainer's first name in a path label; the pre-commit screen ran `validate-maintainer-name-confined` by the book and passed — because the note was still UNTRACKED and `git grep` only scans tracked files. The commit made it tracked; the next `npm test` failed; `main` sat red. A screening tool that passes on the exact class it exists to catch, at the exact moment (pre-commit) the manual habit relies on it, is a lying guard (the D-84/D-169 false-green family).

**The fix:** one flag — `git grep --untracked` — now scans untracked-but-not-ignored files too, while still respecting `.gitignore` (so gitignored tiers stay excluded; they legitimately carry raw content). Two REAL-`git grep` behavior tests against a temp repo pin both halves: an untracked name-carrying file FAILS (status 0), a gitignored one stays excluded (status 1). The `\b<name>` prefix matching (D-103) was never the hole — WHICH FILES got scanned was. Dev-tooling only (ships nothing to users). Note: the validator now catches the name in ANY uncommitted work, not just tracked files — a stronger guarantee than before. _Relates D-310 (the incident this closes), D-102/D-103 (the guard + its matching), Task 122 (the original guard), Task 206 (the sibling capture-vs-screen window), D-318._

## 2026-07-11 — D-317: FIX — Task 213: provenance pointers through the distill chain (the Task-204 refactor made half of it free)

**The gap (D-308, from the Always-On survey):** the rolling-window pipeline was prose-only at each hop — once the 7-day distill unlinked a source day, a `recent.md`/`archive.md` claim had no path back to the session that produced it (lifecycle-map G8; "lossy consolidation is where provenance dies"), and it made Task 210's future deletion-survival search content-fuzzy instead of ID-precise.

**The Task-204 refactor pre-solved the today→recent hop:** `recent.md` is now assembled from per-day `## <date>` sections (the resumable per-day distill), so the source date IS the section header — no work needed beyond an assertion. The remaining gap was recent→archive, where weekly-curate collapses days into `## Week of` sections.

**The fix:** `stampArchiveProvenance(archiveText, sourceDates)` (weekly-curate, pure + exported) deterministically stamps each `## Week of` section with a `<!-- source_days: [YYYY-MM-DD, …] -->` comment from the ACTUAL day-file dates the archivist consumed — added even if the LLM omitted it (the invariant never trusts the model, per the task's own "deterministic post-check" requirement), idempotent on re-run, no-op on empty dates (never fabricates provenance). Forward-only (existing summaries are history, not retro-edited). The pointers resolve to the raw transcript tier (ADR-0010). Pinned by 4 pure-function tests + an integration assertion (a real weekly-curate run stamps the archived week) + the today→recent section-header assertion. G8 softened lossy→lossy-but-TRACEABLE. _Relates the Always-On survey (provenance-preservation), Task 204 (the per-day sections that made today→recent free), Task 210 (the consumer of precise pointers), ADR-0010 (the raw floor), lifecycle-map G8, D-308, D-317._

## 2026-07-11 — D-316: FIX — Task 207: the BOM-unsafe hook-stdin parse generalized to all 11 bins; the REAL-spawn test upgraded it from "no-op" to "guard bypass"

**The class (D-306 generalized):** D-306 fixed the BOM-swallowing `JSON.parse` on the Cursor/Kiro dispatch paths; the standalone Claude-Code hook bins still did the identical `raw.trim()==='' ? {} : JSON.parse(raw)` inline. Latent (Claude Code doesn't BOM its hook stdin), but the same trap: a leading U+FEFF makes the parse throw, the catch swallows it, the bin exits 0 having done nothing.

**The fix:** one `parseHookPayload(raw)` on the canonical `read-hook-stdin.mjs` (stripBom → empty/BOM-only→{} → JSON.parse; malformed STILL throws so the bins' catch blocks own the error path unchanged). The sweep found the class in **11** bins across both trees (packages/cli/bin + plugin/bin — they resolve to the ONE module), not the 4 the task title named; all routed through the helper. A structural test scans every bin for the removed raw-parse shapes (the validate-* posture — a regressed bin fails npm test).

**The severity upgrade (the REAL-spawn test earned its keep):** for `cmk-guard-memory` the BOM class was not a cosmetic no-op — it was a FAIL-OPEN **guard bypass**. Pre-fix, a BOM-prefixed `rm -rf context/memory` payload threw in the parse → the catch fail-opened (exit 0) → the destructive command sailed through. The real-spawn test proves post-fix it BLOCKS (exit 2), byte-identical to the un-BOM'd payload — and a harmless BOM'd payload still allows (no over-block). Without spawning the actual bin on the actual BOM bytes (the Task-221 fixture-corpus discipline), this would have read as "latent hardening" when it was a live-reachable security gap the moment any agent BOMs the guard's payload. _Relates D-306 (the Cursor BOM bug this generalizes), `read-json.mjs stripBom` (the canonical strip), Task 196, the Task-221 live-test discipline, D-316._

## 2026-07-11 — D-315: FIX — Task 206: the commit-proposal excludes the pre-roll `now.md` (the D-304 unmasked-name window closed at the offer, not the buffer)

**The composition bug (D-304, proven live in the Kiro §6/E2 gate):** the privacy screen masks names on the COMMITTED sinks (transcript L1+L3; `today-*.md` via the compress rule), but `now.md` holds the raw turn until its roll — and the ADR-0018 per-turn proposal offered `git add context/`, sweeping the unmasked buffer in if the user accepted first. Two features each correct alone, composing into a leak on the privacy headline.

**The fix — direction (b) of the task's four options:** `buildCommitProposal` (inject-context) excludes `context/sessions/now.md` from BOTH the dirty count (a now.md-only tree proposes NOTHING — the leak shape alone can't even trigger the offer) and the offer text (declaratively instructs staging context/ excluding the pre-roll buffer; git stays agent-run per ADR-0018). **Why (b) over (a)/(c)/(d):** (a) force-roll-before-offer is infeasible at the 500ms inject boundary (a roll is a multi-second Haiku call); (c) L3-screening now.md at write puts an async judge on the per-turn hot path; (d) gitignoring a legitimately-committed tier breaks cross-session continuity. (b) is deterministic, zero-cost, and loses nothing — the roll drains now.md into the screened `today-*.md`, which the NEXT proposal offers.

**Pinned by:** two contract tests (now.md-alone → no proposal; now.md+screened-fact → count excludes it + the exclusion instruction present + no blanket `git add context/`). ADR-0018 carries the dated refinement note (the Decision unchanged — narrows WHAT is proposed, not WHO runs git).

**ADDENDUM — the stress gate caught a Task-205 hermeticity regression riding this PR's gate:** the day-old install preflight (D-314) treated the SHARED `askImpl` seam as consent to scan the REAL system; the binding-fix tests pass a THROWING askImpl sentinel, the scan found this dev machine's genuinely-running server, asked the sentinel, and detonated out of `runInstall` — a nondeterministic environment-dependent failure that two full-suite runs missed and stress run 3 caught. Fix (in this PR): the preflight never touches the real system unless a real TTY is present OR its OWN `findMcpServers` seam is injected, and the whole body is try/caught (best-effort by contract). Two regression tests pin the foreign-askImpl shape + the exploding-find shape. The meta-lesson: a shared consent seam authorizes only the feature it was passed FOR — presence of `askImpl` is not blanket interactivity. _Relates D-304 (the finding), ADR-0018 (refined), ADR-0019/Task 148 (the screen this composes with), Task 205/D-314 (the preflight this hardens), the stress-gate rule (the catch), Task 206, D-315._

## 2026-07-11 — D-314: FIX — Task 205: the half-broken-upgrade class made self-diagnosing (bin boundary + install preflight); the live probe caught a real-payload bug on its FIRST use

**The bug (D-302):** on Windows, `npm install -g` while a `cmk mcp serve` process held the DLL locks could half-break the global — after which EVERY `cmk` command died in the STATIC import chain with a raw `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` stack, before any handler attached (the recorded symptom: `Cannot find module '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk'`).

**Shipped (per the task's fix directions):**
- **(c) The bin boundary:** `bin/cmk.mjs` now imports dynamically inside a try/catch; a module-resolution failure routes through the NEW dependency-free `half-install.mjs` (loads even when the rest of the package doesn't) and prints the 2-step recovery (stop the servers → reinstall) + the data-safety note. REAL-spawn tested: a sandbox bin with an unresolvable import prints the recovery and exits 1; an ordinary error still gets the generic handler (no false half-install claim).
- **(a) The install preflight:** `mcp-procs.mjs` — `findRunningKitMcpServers()` (win32: absolute-System32 PowerShell CIM query, the register-crons S4036 precedent; posix: `ps`), never-throw (a scan failure is silent — an optimization, not a gate), positive identification only (`cmk` + `mcp serve` tokens). `cmk install` names running servers + PIDs + the upgrade caveat and offers an interactive default-NO stop (`taskkill`/`kill` per-pid; the servers reconnect on the next tool call — the documented manual kill, automated).
- **(b) retry-on-EBUSY: N/A-honest** — npm owns the file copy; the kit cannot hook it. **(d) doctor HC: resolved by reasoning** — a half-install that breaks the static import chain prevents `cmk doctor` from RUNNING at all, so the bin boundary IS the detection layer; an HC would be dead code on the exact state it targets.

**The live-test directive earned its keep on FIRST use (the Task-221 discipline, applied same-session):** the unit tests were 17/17 green, and the live probe against THIS machine's actually-running server found **0** — a false negative. Ground-truthing via a raw CIM query showed the real Windows command line quotes EVERY argument (`"node" "…cmk.mjs" "mcp" "serve"`), which the `\bmcp\s+serve\b` matcher can't see — the D-305/D-306 real-payload class, a THIRD instance. Fixed (`mcp["']?\s+["']?serve`), and the REAL captured command line committed as the test fixture (the fixture-corpus playbook technique). Without the live probe this shipped green-and-broken on the only platform that matters for the bug.

_Relates D-302 (the filing), `P-aLLW62HD`/`P-YFBTYUPQ` (the incident memories), Task 221 + the CLAUDE.md cut-gate-parity rule (the discipline that caught the matcher bug), D-305/D-306 (the sibling real-payload class), QUICKSTART §9 (the docs half), D-314._

## 2026-07-11 — D-313: DECISION + FIX — v0.5.1 opens: Tasks 203 (daily-distill starvation) + 204 (incremental-resumable jobs, ADR-0020) shipped together; + the live-test-toward-cut-gate-parity directive

**Context.** The user opened v0.5.1 ("we can start on version v0.5.1, right?") on autopilot, with two standing directives: (a) *"do live tests as much as you can … so we don't have to wait for me to do the cut gate to find problems"* + *"maybe some deep research on it"*; (b) autopilot with stop-only-for-problems.

**Task 204 — the incremental-resumable principle (ADR-0020, the NEW cross-cutting law).** Long jobs must be built so a run killed at 80% persists the 80% and RESUMES — never lose completed work, never re-do it. The resume point is DERIVED FROM ARTIFACTS (ADR-0002: no new sentinel file — the two-writer hazard). A third strategy alongside bound-input (D-173) + derive-from-artifacts. Shipped: ADR-0020 + the CLAUDE.md "Engineering discipline" checklist + `daily-distill` refactored as the reference beneficiary.

**Task 203 — daily-distill starvation (D-298), three fixes:**
1. **`daily-distill` resumable refactor (the 204 reference):** was all-or-nothing (whole 7-day corpus → one `compress()` → overwrite `recent.md`); now distills ONE `today-*.md` per call, persists `today-<date>.distilled.md` immediately, resumes from artifact mtime, assembles `recent.md` from the per-day artifacts. A run killed after 3 of 7 keeps 3; the next run does 4. **Live-verified end-to-end** (killed after 2 of 5 → banks 2, recent.md fresh → resume 3, skip 2).
2. **HC-10 false-green fix ("watch the watchmen"):** HC-10 reported PASS on a fresh heartbeat alone — but the heartbeat records at task START, so a cron that fired + died mid-distill five nights running showed PASS while `recent.md` went 5 days stale. Now HC-10 ALSO checks `recent.md` freshness: fresh-heartbeat + stale-output = the starvation tell → FAIL. Test seeds the exact broken state.
3. **Cascade-starvation fix:** `runLazyCompress`'s `stale-now` verdict PERMANENTLY shadowed `stale-daily` (a busy repo's now.md always has content → stale-now won every pass → daily distill never ran). Now after the now-roll drains now.md, staleness is re-derived and the daily/weekly work that's now due cascades in the same pass.
4. **`register-crons` WakeToRun:** added `-WakeToRun` to the existing best-effort `Set-ScheduledTask` (alongside `StartWhenAvailable`) so the machine WAKES at 23:00 to run the distill instead of the cron dying on a sleeping laptop. (Host-scheduler registration only takes effect when the USER runs `cmk register-crons` — not run on the maintainer's machine per the autopilot host-scheduler stop rule.)

**The two-pass review earned its keep (four real bugs the skill-review caught that self-review + green tests missed — all fixed inline before merge, D-313):**
1. **Double temporal-sweep:** the cascade-to-weekly path ran the temporal sweep TWICE (weeklyCurate sweeps internally + the post-delegation sweep fired because the `!== 'weekly-curate'` guard only knew the PRIMARY verdict). Fix: a `cascadedToWeekly` flag extends the guard to the cascade. Test asserts exactly one sweep.
2. **`maxOutputBytes` cap unenforced + a FALSE comment:** the code claimed "the final consolidation pass re-caps the whole" — there was no such pass; `assembleRecent` just concatenated, so `recent.md` could exceed its documented bounded-summary cap (the 512-byte per-day floor defeats it for many-section weeks). Fix: `assembleRecent` now re-caps by dropping OLDEST `## <date>` sections first; comment corrected. Test pins the cap.
3. **Empty-artifact masks a re-distill:** a backend returning empty/whitespace (a soft hiccup) wrote a blank `.distilled.md` → `dayNeedsDistill` saw it as "done" → the day was silently dropped forever. Fix: only bank + count a NON-empty result; empty leaves no artifact → re-distills next run. Test pins it.
4. **Orphaned `.distilled.md`:** weekly-curate deleted old `today-*.md` but not their distilled siblings → ~1 orphan/day accumulating (disk-only, gitignored, no corruption — verified they never re-enter recent.md). Fix: the deletion loop also removes the sibling artifact (best-effort). Test pins it.

**The live-test directive → durable process (the user's (a)).** Ran deep web research ([research note](../research/2026-07-11-faithful-live-test-automation.md)): the gap is a test-TAXONOMY gap (Google small/medium/large — hermetic tests are DESIGNED blind to boundary bugs), not a quantity gap; the playbook = fixture corpus of real captured payloads → property-based weird-input → "watch the watchmen" → fault-inject kill-mid-run → OS matrix → broaden live-verify. This shipped as: the CLAUDE.md "Live-test toward cut-gate parity" binding rule + **Task 221** (the staged harness build-out). 203/204 already applied two of the playbook's techniques (the HC-10 watch-the-watchmen test + the killed-at-80% fault-inject resumability test + a real live-module probe).

_Relates D-298/D-299 (the bug + principle), ADR-0020 (the resumable law), ADR-0002 (the derive-from-artifacts constraint), the CLAUDE.md "Long jobs … resumable" + "Live-test toward cut-gate parity" rules, Task 221 (the harness follow-up), the 2026-07-11 research note, D-313._

## 2026-07-10 — D-312: NOTE + FIX — the codebase-wide code-review sweep (the user's ask): 6 review agents (5 clusters + security), findings VERIFIED before action, 3 confirmed bugs fixed + 5 filed

**The ask (the user):** "run all the code-review skills we have on all our code." Run: 6 parallel Sonnet review agents — core write path / recall-search / hooks-compression / learn-loop-trust / install-agents-CLI + a dedicated security pass — each told to VERIFY before reporting (read callers, check for a pinning test, confirm reachability) and to skip already-filed bugs. Then I independently re-verified every load-bearing finding against source before touching code (the "did you check?" discipline — one review claim was overstated and got downgraded on verification, below).

**The through-line the sweep found (3 agents converged on it):** Poison_Guard is ONE well-built chokepoint with structurally-identical UNSCREENED side doors — content that is LLM-generated (summaries, persona classifications) or externally-sourced (imported files, trust escalations) is implicitly trusted and skips the screen direct user/assistant writes get. This is the "separately-correct-jointly-broken" class the kit's own CLAUDE.md names, on the security surface.

**FIXED THIS BATCH (confirmed + TDD, red-first):**
1. **`write-fact.mjs` screened `body` but not `title`** (Blocking) — `cmk remember --title "ghp_…"` / `mk_remember` title param landed a real-shaped secret verbatim in committed frontmatter + INDEX.md, zero test coverage. Fix: screen `title`+`body` concatenation. Test: a secret in the title → rejected, no file written.
2. **`import-anthropic-memory.mjs` had ZERO screening** (High, security finding 3) — laundered Anthropic's native auto-memory file (a different tool context) straight into committed `MEMORY.md` with no Poison_Guard, no home-path sanitize (the sibling `import-claude-md` does both). Fix: home-path-sanitize + Poison_Guard each imported bullet; a hit drops that bullet (logged, redacted), counts as skipped.
3. **`judge-signals.mjs::judgeUserPrompt` resolved EVERY pending expectation project-wide on any correction** (High, learn-loop finding 1) — a correction about today's login fix locked a two-day-old deploy prediction to MISS/REVERSAL (the sticky verdicts). The smoking gun: the `dampenAll` call right above it ALREADY scopes to `TURN_WINDOW_MS`; the expectation loop forgot to. The over-mutation class the kit's own test discipline exists to catch. Fix: gate the loop on `nowMs - expTs <= TURN_WINDOW_MS` (fail-closed on undated). Tests: stale expectation untouched + in-window still resolves.

Plus 5 stale-comment fixes (the internal-reference-drift rule): feedback-screen's "4 callers" → 5 (named); memory-write's "KNOWN GAP … Task 151.12 deferred" → CLOSED (both writers already dampen); auto-extract's stale "25,000 ms under the 30s ceiling" → the accurate detached-90s note; daily-distill's "atomic temp+rename" comment → the truthful direct-write-under-single-writer note.

**FILED (need design judgment, not a blind fix — the honest split):**
- **Task 216** — the shared `screenBeforeCommittedWrite()` helper for the REMAINING unscreened committed-write sites (weekly-curate/daily-distill summaries, transcript-promotion which screens only PII not secrets, persona-review queue, mk_trust/lessons-promote re-screen on escalation). v0.5.1 rider — one helper, ~5 sites.
- **Task 217** — harden the delete-guardrail (regex-only; misses language-level deletes `shutil.rmtree`/`fs.rmSync`, the `Write` tool entirely, encoded commands, and its own `.claude/settings.json`). Design-first (over-block/under-block balance), v0.5.x security depth — it's defense-in-depth over git-recoverability + audit, not the primary guarantee.
- **Task 218** — MCP server index freshness. **DOWNGRADED on verification:** the review's "invisible for the whole SESSION" framing was overstated — `mk_remember`→`writeFact` DOES reindex on write (write-fact.mjs:456), and WAL-shared-db means the server's boot connection should see committed writes. The real (narrower) gap is EXTERNALLY-changed files (another window/hook/CLI write). Task says REPRODUCE FIRST, don't build blind.
- **Task 219** — add `busy_timeout` to `openIndexDb` (design §16.34 was proposed but NEVER shipped — the audit expected it in Task 31/146; it isn't there). One-line + a concurrent-writer test. v0.5.1 rider.
- **Task 220** — duplicate managed-block blindness in install/uninstall (non-global regex → second block orphaned on upgrade, survives uninstall; live-reproduced). v0.5.1 rider.

**Verified CLEAN (recorded so the confidence is durable):** command-injection via argv (every LLM-backend spawn delivers content over stdin, never argv — the Windows cmd-quoting layer never sees attacker content); path traversal (every content→filename collapses to `[a-z0-9-]`, every MCP id gates on ID_PATTERN); the mk_forget confirm-token scheme (id+body-bound, unforgeable without the preview); the learn-loop invariants (single trust-mutation gate, 5 callers zero bypass; judgments never rank; hot path never reordered by trust_score); the timeout-composition discipline (§8.5) at every checked site; the recursion guard across all backends. The hooks/session cluster came back essentially clean (only 2 stale comments). No HC-10-style false-green siblings found.

**Meta — the verification earned its keep twice:** the write-path `--title` finding was real and I confirmed it with a red test before fixing; the MCP "invisible all session" finding was OVERSTATED and got downgraded to needs-repro after I traced write-fact.mjs:456 (the reindex-on-write the review missed). Report findings faithfully in BOTH directions — confirm the real ones, downgrade the overstated ones. _Relates D-311 (the same-day full-repo audit this pairs with), the 6 review agent reports (session artifacts), CLAUDE.md's Poison_Guard + delete-guardrail + internal-reference-drift rules, Tasks 216–220 (filed), D-312._

## 2026-07-10 — D-311: NOTE + FIX — the full-repo audit (the user's ask): every shipped task verified against live code (131/131 clean, 0 suspect), the open-task fired-trigger walk run, the docs drift-checked — and the found drift fixed in one batch

**The ask (the user):** verify shipped tasks REALLY shipped (code exists + works), verify every open task is laned-or-triggered, audit the docs. Run: 5 parallel verification agents (shipped ranges 1–79 / 80–149 / 150–208; the open-task disposition walk; the docs health pass), then a fix batch.

**Shipped-task verification — CLEAN.** 131 `[x]` top-level tasks audited against the live repo (named files/functions/tests/verbs greped, not trusted from memory; the suite green at 2833 that day): **0 SUSPECT**. ~13 VERIFIED-EVOLVED (documented renames/relocations/supersessions — e.g. `merged_into`→`superseded_by`, INSTALL-*.md retired per ADR-0005, Task 167's fix shipped as `compaction-state.mjs`); 9 DOCS-ONLY (checkpoints). The learn-loop cluster (190–193) specifically verified as WIRED (imports + call sites in the hook path), not dead files. "We said we shipped it but didn't" — not found anywhere.

**Open-task walk — 45 open tasks, all pass the validator; the judgment layer found and this batch fixed:**
- **tasks.md's own "Current state" header was FIVE releases stale** (v0.3.5-latest / Task-164-in-flight) — the exact section BOOTSTRAP.md tells a fresh session to trust. REFRESHED (live state + the D-309 queue at top; old bullets kept as history). The proposed structural guard (assert the header's newest version-string against CHANGELOG's) is noted under Task 186's scope for the validator-consolidation decision.
- **Fired-trigger re-verdicts recorded** (the D-267 walk): **141** — phase (a) was already shipped v0.3.x (D-260) and its entry now says so; phase (b)'s "or the v0.5.0 cut" clause FIRED → verdict: stays REJECTED-deferred (D-162 perf stands), trigger refreshed to npm-12-GA + a first real failure report. **186** — its "v0.4.4 cut" trigger passed; refreshed to the now-CONCRETE "decide in the Task 214 (v0.5.1) validator-suite touch." **174** — flagged that its trigger ("next daily-distill touch") FIRES at v0.5.1's 203/204; decide there. **184/189** — already re-verdicted at the v0.5.0-cut sweep (KEEP); their entries now carry that verdict instead of pointing at passed sweeps.
- **Stale v0.4.x operative labels re-labeled** (v0.4 is closed): 174, 175 (seam dependency met), 177/180 (research lanes made version-agnostic), 127 (trigger: real team-demand or the v0.6 boundary), 51 (given its own explicit disposition — was free-riding on the shipped v0.3.0 bucket).
- **False alarms (recorded so they aren't re-flagged):** Task 179 is `[~]`-resolved already (agent misread); 189/184 were sweep-verdicted; README DOES carry the Updating note (line ~125) so Task 162's doc claim holds.

**Docs audit — drift found + fixed:** ARCHITECTURE.md described semantic search as "not shipped" (false since v0.3.0 — corrected, decision-trail note kept) and had NO learn-loop or privacy screen (added: a Layer 4.5 learn-loop section + the ADR-0019 line — summaries with pointers, D-228 honored). memory-lifecycle-map.md gained edges 18–21 (recall-log / judgment / judge / feedback-screen). QUICKSTART's `0.2.x` version example made version-agnostic. Verified CLEAN (no fix needed): glossary, README features, CLI.md/MCP.md (validator-backed), the three per-agent docs (parallel), HEALTH-CHECKS.

**Filed:** **Task 215** — the nightly `cmk-daily-distill` schtask pops a visible node.exe console window on Windows (the user saw it live at 23:01; run itself healthy, LastTaskResult 0). v0.5.1 rider; NOT the Task-81 spawn-flag class — a schtask registration-shape fix, live-verify against a real trigger.

**Deliberately NOT done (proposals surfaced to the user instead):** splitting/archiving tasks.md's shipped v0.1.0 body (~850 lines) into a frozen archive file — real navigational win but reference-breakage churn, the user decides; a design.md table-of-contents (additive, low risk, no D-228 issue); DECISION-LOG/build-log splits REJECTED (append-only logs, size is intrinsic, splitting fragments D-N references).

_Relates D-47 (the audit discipline this reruns at full scale), D-267 (the fired-trigger walk executed), D-309/D-310 (the same-day context), Task 186 (the header-staleness guard candidate), Task 215 (filed), the 5 agent reports (session artifacts)._

## 2026-07-10 — D-310: BUG + FIX — the D-308 sweep note leaked the maintainer's first name (a `<name>wiki` path label); TWO process holes let it ship: the name-guard skips UNTRACKED files, and the CI watch latched onto the wrong workflow while CI itself failed

**The leak.** The research-sweep note's Pointers section cited the 6G article's local capture by its real path — a personal-wiki dir whose name embeds the maintainer's first name (`<name>wiki/raw/`). Exactly the prefix form the D-103 hardening anticipated (`<name>wiki` is D-103's own example); the PATTERN was right, the leak shipped anyway because of hole #1.

**Hole #1 — the screen ran on an untracked file.** The pre-commit screen ran `validate-maintainer-name-confined` by the book → OK — but the validator's `git grep` scans TRACKED files only, and the note was untracked at screen time. The commit (1020e98) made it tracked; the next local `npm test` failed. **Filed Task 214** (add `--untracked` to the validator's grep + a red-first test; v0.5.1 rider, do early — a guard that passes on the class it exists to catch is a lying guard).

**Hole #2 — the CI watch picked the wrong run.** After the push, `gh run watch $(gh run list --limit 1 ...)` latched onto one of the SIX workflows the push triggered (Security/CodeQL/canonicalize-parity registered first, all green) — while the actual **CI workflow FAILED on the validator** and `main` sat red until this session's next local run surfaced it. The watch-CI rule's EXECUTION was wrong, not the rule: watch the CI workflow BY NAME. CLAUDE.md's watch-CI rule gains sub-rule (c) in this batch.

**FIX (this batch):** the note's path label → "the maintainer's local wiki capture (personal-wiki `raw/` dir)"; validator green locally; pushed with `main` watched by workflow name to green. **The history residue, honestly:** commit 1020e98's blob retains the string in git history. Assessed + accepted (no history rewrite): the maintainer's first name is ALREADY public on every commit via the git author identity, which the Name-privacy rule explicitly scopes OUT ("the user controls their own git config") — the residue adds zero new exposure, and a `filter-repo` force-push would break clones for nothing (the Task 96 calculus). The name-guard's job is the TRACKED TREE, and that is restored.

**Meta.** Same family as D-304 (a window between capture and screen) and D-84 (false-green: the check that doesn't check). The screen was run in good faith at the wrong moment against a tool with a blind spot — the fix is structural (Task 214), not more prose. _Relates D-102/D-103 (the guard), Task 214 (the structural fix), Task 122 (the guard's origin), D-308 (the note), the CLAUDE.md watch-CI rule (sub-rule c added this batch), `P-XVB7HWGJ` (the prior watch-CI precedent this extends: that one didn't watch at all; this one watched the wrong run)._

## 2026-07-10 — D-309: DECISION — the v0.5 release QUEUE: expected digits for every laned task (the user's "real versions, not v0.5.x" call), + the Codex v0.4.6 → v0.5.2 re-label; D-157 STANDS

**Context.** After D-308 laned the sweep's tasks as "v0.5.x," the user: *"can we decide on real versions? saying 'v0.5.x' is too vague."* The tension: **D-157 is SETTLED** — the patch digit tracks shipments, not pre-numbering, because pre-numbered slots demonstrably slip (Cursor lost its locked v0.4.1; the Task-173 security patch jumped into v0.4.2 and slid 151 to v0.4.3). Surfaced to the user rather than silently violated.

**Decision: commit the ORDERED QUEUE with EXPECTED digits; D-157 stands.** The order + content are the commitment; the digit is "expected" and re-stamps only if something jumps the queue (the v0.4.2 precedent is the named hazard, not a hypothetical). This kills the "v0.5.x" vagueness without re-opening D-157. The queue (recorded in RELEASE-PLAN, the authoritative lane file):

1. **v0.5.1** — Tasks 203+204 (headline) + riders 205 / 206 / 207 / 213. (Already committed, unchanged.)
2. **v0.5.2 — the Codex adapter** (Task 196 tail) + Task 165 (rides the same kiro/agent surface).
3. **v0.5.3 — Phase 2, the learn-loop payoff** — Task 194 + batch riders 209 / 211 / 212.
4. **v0.5.4 — the governance batch** — Task 96 + Task 210 (+ Task 95 IF its needs-design gate has cleared; its gate, not the queue, decides).

**The Codex re-label (the stale-digit fix):** Codex was still labeled "v0.4.6" from the D-257 sequencing — but v0.5.0 shipped AHEAD of it (the D-286 re-order), and tagging a 0.4.6 AFTER 0.5.0 would misorder npm `latest`. Codex's expected slot is now **v0.5.2**; the old labels are preserved in Task 196/165 + RELEASE-PLAN per the decision-trail rule. Codex-before-Phase-2 (not after) because: the breadth commitment is demand-backed (D-257, the maintainer's friends), and it buys Phase 1 live mileage before Phase 2 tunes on Phase 1's signals ("after Phase 1 proves live" is 194's own gate).

**Not releases:** Task 208 (the Cursor interactive gate) is a gate RUN riding whatever slot is current when tokens refresh (~2026-07-24); trigger-gated tasks (212 if it fires early, 47/48, etc.) ride the slot open when their trigger fires.

_Relates D-157 (stands — surfaced, not violated), D-308 (the filings this numbers), D-257/D-286 (the sequencing history behind the Codex re-label), D-248 (lane-or-trigger), RELEASE-PLAN (the authoritative queue)._

## 2026-07-10 — D-308: DECISION — the five-source memory-research sweep: file Tasks 209–213 + three fold-ins, lane them (Phase-2 batch / v0.5.1 riders / governance slot), reject the training-class ideas

**Context.** The user brought five research sources (4 arXiv PDFs + 1 article + the Fergana-Labs `stash` repo) with the ask: study them ALL against the kit and steal what makes it better. Each source was deep-read in full (PDFs incl. appendices; the repo's sources) by a dedicated subagent, mapped against the live backlog (SYSTEM-MAP §6, the open tasks, the D-248 dispositions). Full findings: [`docs/research/2026-07-10-memory-research-sweep.md`](../research/2026-07-10-memory-research-sweep.md); SOURCES.md carries the five new entries.

**The full-research-base rule earned its keep:** MEMORA (arXiv 2602.03315) was in the batch — and was ALREADY fully disposed by the 2026-07-03 review (Tasks 198/199 + folds into 176/178/95/149). Without checking the research base first, this sweep would have re-filed cue anchors as a "new" idea. The independent re-read CONFIRMED the 2026-07-03 verdicts and added one datapoint: MEMORA's own Fig-2 shows GRPO-training their retriever buys +1.2pt overall and LOSES multi-hop, while their structural pieces carry 0.653→0.849 — structure-beats-training in their own numbers (the kit's no-training posture, externally validated).

**Filed (new tasks, each with its lane per the D-248 discipline):**
- **Task 209 — state-labeled recall** (`[current]`/`[superseded]` on facts serialized into search results + snapshot; A-TMA arXiv 2607.01935's case study: identical evidence flips wrong→correct on labels alone; deterministic projection of the SHIPPED Task-66 state fields, hot path stays enum-ordered per §20.3). → **v0.5.x, rides the Task 194 Phase-2 batch.**
- **Task 210 — deletion-propagation guarantee** (a tombstoned fact verifiably gone from FTS5/`recent.md`/`archive.md`/snapshot; the Always-On survey arXiv 2606.30306's #1 field gap — rollback 27/435; AOEP's obligation-vs-negative-invariant two-sided test discipline adopted). → **v0.5.x, rides Task 96's governance slot.**
- **Task 211 — rule-based query state-view gate** (current/historical/transition/neutral wordlist classifier biasing which STATE surfaces; the un-deferred 4-view cut of §16.18's 7-mode classifier — A-TMA proves the rule-based version needs no classifier infra, killing the deferral's cost premise; the §16.18 note carries the pivot annotation, original deferral preserved). → **v0.5.x, rides the 194 batch.**
- **Task 212 — memory-health behavioral dashboard** (writes-per-search / empty-search / redundant-write / snapshot cost from the SHIPPED recall-log + audit log; AutoMem arXiv 2607.01224's Fig-4 indicator set — their optimized agents' writes-per-search fell 54–72%). → **Trigger: fires when 194's tuning starts (it IS 194's before/after instrumentation) or the next doctor-surface touch.** Sibling of 189 (process-health vs user-ROI), no embeddings needed.
- **Task 213 — provenance pointers through the distill chain** (per-section source-session tags in today/recent/archive; the survey's provenance-preservation invariant; softens G8; makes 210's survival-search ID-precise). → **v0.5.1, rides 203/204** (they refactor the exact distill code).

**Folded into existing tasks (no new numbers):** Task 70 gains **70.5** (source-trust tiers at ingestion — user-stated vs tool-output before committed authority; the survey's evidence-before-belief, extending the D-303 origin-routing seed); Task 95 gains the AutoMem meta-LLM scaffold self-audit scope candidate + stash curator prompt references; Task 189 cross-refs 212; Task 199 gains stash's dated-`## Updates` contradiction-append reference.

**Validation (no build, recorded for positioning):** the survey's AOEP pilot found extracted-fact stores (Mem0-style) score WORSE on governance than raw storage because extraction flattens the envelope — independent, citable validation of ADR-0002 (markdown + visible frontmatter); its Fig-3 shows governance coverage rising 12%→46% (2023→2026) — the kit's audit/tombstone/journal investment is ahead of the field's turn. stash positioning: the kit wins zero-infra / write-time screening / per-repo tiers / same-turn distillation; stash wins cross-source reach + team sharing.

**Rejected (the sweep's training/white-box class — none re-openable without new evidence):** β-VAE latent handover + `inputs_embeds` injection (the 6G article — NOTHING transfers, black-box agents + no-training; recorded in the sweep note so it isn't re-read); GRPO/LoRA retrieval or memory-specialist training (MEMORA/AutoMem); A-TMA's trained Sentry embedding classifier (the temporal sweep already has the cheap-gate→judge shape). None of the filed items violate the constraint edges: 209 labels-never-ranks, 211 is a deterministic bucket bias not a score blend, §20.3's hot-path rule and the §5 scale-floor stay untouched.

_Relates D-307 (the v0.5.0 tag this lands after), D-248 (lane-or-trigger discipline applied to every filing), D-266/D-259 (the MEMORA-derived precedents confirmed), ADR-0017 (the Phase-2 batch these ride), the sweep note + SOURCES.md entries (same batch)._

## 2026-07-10 — D-307: DECISION — tag v0.5.0 NOW; defer the Cursor INTERACTIVE cut-gate to a token-refresh trigger (the user's call)

**Context.** The v0.5.0 tag was held under D-292 (all three agent gates must pass, so the README's Kiro/Cursor claims aren't a lie). This session closed the Kiro gate (§2/§5/§6 all live-PASS) and found + fixed THREE Cursor-Windows capture bugs — D-305 (`/c:/` workspace_roots path), D-306 (the UTF-8 BOM breaking `JSON.parse`, the real killer), and the shared `parseHookStdin`. All merged; the repacked global `cmk` was **live-verified on the EXACT bytes Cursor 3.5.17 sends** (BOM-prefixed `/c:/` payloads → full bi-turn `now.md`, user=1 assistant=1, preference captured). Then the maintainer's free-tier Cursor tokens ran out mid-gate (~14-day refresh).

**Decision (the user, 2026-07-10): tag v0.5.0 now; defer the full interactive Cursor gate.** Rationale — the D-292 lie-guard is SATISFIED without the interactive run: "Cursor capture works" is now a TRUE, verified claim (the bugs are fixed and the core capture is proven at the payload boundary through the real global bin), not an unverified assertion. What's deferred is the EXHAUSTIVE in-IDE gate (R-inject / E1 wedge / E2 privacy / E3 learn-loop), which is *additional confidence*, not *correctness* — the correctness question ("does capture work") is answered yes. Holding the tag 14 days would delay a done release (Kiro capture + the Task-148 privacy screen + the Tasks 190-193 learn-loop, all live-gated) and risk context loss.

**Honest-not-silent (the decision-trail + lazy-framing rules).** The deferral is RECORDED, not hidden: **Task 208** carries the owed interactive gate with a NAMED trigger (D-307: fires on Cursor token refresh, ~2026-07-24), and the release note states Cursor capture is verified at the payload level with the full interactive gate scheduled next session. This is the difference between a stated deferral and a disclaimer that ships a latent gap — the former is the process working.

**What ships in v0.5.0** (all live-verified this session or prior): D-303 Kiro capture fix + the B1 transcript-parity; the Task-148 privacy screen (Kiro §6/E2 PASS); the Tasks 190-193 learn-loop (Kiro §6/E3 PASS); D-305 + D-306 Cursor capture fixes (payload-boundary verified). Deferred to v0.5.1: Task 206 (pre-roll now.md name window), Task 207 (Claude-bin BOM hardening), Task 208 (Cursor interactive gate). _Relates D-292 (the 3-agent gate this satisfies), D-305/D-306 (the Cursor fixes), Task 208 (the deferred gate), the Kiro §2/§5/§6 completion (the parallel bar Cursor's payload-verify meets at the core level)._

## 2026-07-09 — D-306: BUG + FIX (v0.5.0, tag-blocking) — the TRUE Cursor-Windows capture killer: Cursor prepends a UTF-8 BOM to the hook stdin, so `JSON.parse` threw and EVERY hook silently no-op'd

**Class:** the same "real agent payload ≠ our adapter's assumption" family as D-303/D-305, but this is the ROOT — D-305 (the `/c:/` path fix) was necessary but only mattered for the rare payloads that parsed at all; **most never parsed.**

**Symptom (Cursor gate, live).** After D-305 merged + the global repacked, a real Cursor session STILL captured nothing: `now.md` and both transcripts had **0 user headings** (all assistant), and every auto-extract logged `nothing_durable` / `rich_facts_written:0` (the `/version` fact was `low_trust_discarded`, demoted-from-medium, origin:assistant — the D-303 shape: no user turn → everything low-trust → dropped). The hooks FIRED (Cursor's panel showed `afterAgentResponse 1423ms`, exit 0) but produced nothing.

**Root cause (ground-truth, via an instrumented probe — after research + a killed hypothesis).** I first hypothesized `CMK_BACKEND_SPAWN` leaking (the recursion guard) — a plausible "exit 0, no write" cause from OUR code, but I hadn't verified it. A sonnet research pass (Cursor forum, staff-confirmed) found the real class: Cursor-Windows hooks get malformed `/c:/…` roots AND — the decisive part — a probe that dumped the raw stdin showed **every payload begins with a UTF-8 BOM (`﻿`, U+FEFF)**. `runCursorHook` did `JSON.parse(raw)` with no BOM strip → THROWS → the `catch` set `payload = {}` → `hook_event_name` is `''` → unknown event → no-op, exit 0, empty stdout. Proven with a two-line differential: **WITH BOM → `stdout=""` (no-op); NO BOM → `stdout="{"continue":true}"` (works).** The env dump ALSO killed the `CMK_BACKEND_SPAWN` theory (not present) and confirmed `CURSOR_PROJECT_DIR` arrives clean.

**Why this is the ROOT, not D-305.** `beforeSubmitPrompt` (the USER turn) died on the BOM every time → the user prompt never reached the transcript → `afterAgentResponse`'s bi-turn extraction saw an assistant-only turn → `nothing_durable`. The `/c:/` path (D-305) only bit the *subset* of payloads that got past `JSON.parse` — which, with the BOM, was **none** from real Cursor. Both fixes are needed; this one unblocks the whole path.

**FIX.** A shared `parseHookStdin(raw)` helper: strip a leading BOM via a **codePoint check** (`charCodeAt(0) === 0xFEFF`, not an invisible literal an editor could drop), then `JSON.parse`, returning `{}` for empty/unparseable (the clean-no-op contract). Applied at BOTH stdin-parse sites — `runCursorHook` (Cursor) and the Kiro `postToolUse` path (defensive; Kiro reads event from argv so it wasn't the live victim, but the parse site is identical). Kiro's main path is unaffected (argv/env/transcript, no stdin JSON).

**TDD + live proof.** New failing test (`a UTF-8 BOM prefix … still parses + routes`) red → green: a BOM-prefixed `beforeSubmitPrompt` now runs capturePrompt AND returns `{continue:true}`. End-to-end: BOM-prefixed `beforeSubmitPrompt`+`afterAgentResponse` (exactly as Cursor sends) → `now.md` with **user=1, assistant=1** + the httpx preference captured. Full suite re-running.

**Meta — the discipline that got here.** The user's push — *"why are you hypothesizing? is anything according to research? docs? web?"* — is what turned a guessed `CMK_BACKEND_SPAWN` theory into a researched + probe-confirmed root cause. The instrument-don't-hypothesize rule (diagnosing-bugs Phase 1) + primary-source research + a differential test. _Relates D-305 (the path fix this completes), D-303 (the sibling Kiro capture bug + the low-trust-demote shape), Task 196 (the Cursor hook)._

## 2026-07-09 — D-305: BUG + FIX (v0.5.0, tag-blocking) — Cursor auto-capture silently wrote to a dead path on Windows: `workspace_roots` arrives as `/c:/…` (leading slash before the drive letter)

**Class:** an agent's real payload shape our adapter didn't handle — the same FAMILY as D-303 (Kiro), a different mechanism (path format, not a missing field). Found the same way: driving the REAL agent in the cut-gate, not unit tests.

**Symptom (Cursor §1/§2 gate, live).** The Cursor hooks were wired and FIRING (Cursor's own Hooks panel showed `afterAgentResponse 1423ms cmd.exe /c cmk cursor-hook` in its execution log), yet `context/sessions/now.md` stayed empty after multiple turns and 0 auto-extract facts landed. Explicit `mk_remember` worked (the `.venv` bullet landed) — because MCP loads live and doesn't depend on the project-root path; only the HOOK capture/inject/observe legs were dead.

**Root cause (ground-truth captured, not guessed).** I initially mis-blamed a stale session (WRONG — the user's screenshot proved the hooks were loaded and firing). A stdin-logging probe wired into the gate folder captured the EXACT payload Cursor 3.5.17 sends on Windows:
`"workspace_roots":["/c:/Temp/cursor-gate-v050c"]` — a **leading slash before the drive letter**, forward-slashed. `runCursorHook` took `workspace_roots[0]` as the project root verbatim; `path.join("/c:/Temp/proj", "context", "sessions")` on Windows yields `\c:\Temp\proj\context\sessions` — a bogus path resolved off the process cwd. So `captureTurn`/`injectContext`/`observeEdit` all wrote to a garbage location, and `now.md` in the real project was never touched. macOS/Linux send a normal `/Users/…` root → unaffected.

**The false trails I walked (and how they were killed).** Four times my repros "showed a mangled path" — every one was MY OWN bash/`node -e` backslash-escaping error, NOT the kit. I only got ground truth by (a) capturing the real Cursor stdin via a probe, and (b) authoring the repro in a pure `.mjs` file so no shell ate the backslashes. Feeding both the real `/c:/…` form AND valid `C:\…`/`C:/…` forms proved the core is fine — ONLY the leading-slash-drive form breaks.

**FIX.** `normalizeCursorRoot(root)` strips a leading slash/backslash run that directly precedes a `<letter>:` drive (`/^[/\\]+([A-Za-z]:)/` → `$1`), applied at the single `workspace_roots` read site in `runCursorHook`. Passthrough-safe: `/home/u/proj` (no drive → no match), `C:\Temp\proj` and `C:/Temp/proj` (no leading slash → no match) are untouched. Kiro is unaffected (it uses `process.cwd()`, not a payload root). Claude Code unaffected (uses `cwd`/`transcript_path`).

**TDD + live proof.** New failing test (`normalizes Cursor-on-Windows /c:/… workspace_roots`) red → green, plus a POSIX+Windows passthrough guard. End-to-end: fed the EXACT captured Cursor payload into `cmk cursor-hook` → `now.md` now lands at the correct project root with the assistant turn. Full suite re-running.

**Meta.** Third live-gate find of the "real agent payload ≠ our adapter's assumption" class (Kiro USER_PROMPT-empty D-303; Kiro transcript B1; now Cursor `/c:/` D-305). Each was invisible to green unit tests and surfaced only by driving the actual agent — the "live-test every task / docs-correct ≠ fires" rule. _Relates D-303 (the sibling Kiro capture fix), Task 196 (the Cursor hook), the cross-agent adapter seam (D-180)._

## 2026-07-09 — D-304: ISSUE (filed Task 206, v0.5.1) — the per-turn "commit context/ now?" prompt can commit an UNMASKED name from the pre-roll `now.md` (found live in the v0.5.0 Kiro cut-gate)

**Class:** separately-correct-jointly-broken, on the privacy surface. Two shipped features, each correct alone, compose into a leak path.

**What the Kiro §6/E2 gate found.** The privacy screen masks names on both COMMITTED sinks — the transcript (L1 `«EMAIL»` + L3 judge `«NAME»`) and the daily summary `today-{date}.md` (compress-prompt rule #7, the Task-148 review-I1 name-defense). But `context/sessions/now.md` is a git-TRACKED tier that holds the raw turn between capture and the roll: it gets L1 masking (email/phone/username) but NOT name screening — names need the L3 judge / compressor, and neither touches `now.md` until it ROLLS. So a bare personal name sits unmasked in `now.md` during the pre-roll window.

**Proven live.** Cold-open session; the assistant echoed the git identity (`Alex Personname` / `alex.personname@gmail.com`) into a turn. Result: committed transcript AND `today-*.md` both correctly showed `«NAME»`/`«EMAIL»` — but `now.md` held the raw `Alex Personname`. Forcing `cmk roll --scope now` drained it and the resulting `today-*.md` was clean. **So the screen WORKS; the gap is only the pre-roll window.**

**Why it's a real exposure.** ADR-0018's per-turn commit prompt — the kit's OWN Kiro assistant offered *"commit context/ now?"* on every turn of the gate. A user who accepts before the roll → `git add context/` commits the unmasked name. The roll masks; the prompt offers convenience; together they open a leak on the exact feature (privacy) that headlines v0.5.0.

**Decision (the user, 2026-07-09): file as v0.5.1, NOT a v0.5.0 blocker.** Rationale: the actual committed artifacts (transcript, `today-*.md`) are correctly screened; the roll masks `now.md` when it fires; this is a narrow behavioral window (raw name in a tracked-but-transient buffer + a user accepting the per-turn commit before the roll fires), medium severity — not a broken screen, and NOT a regression from the D-303 fix (the D-303 fix's own now.md write is already L1-masked; this is the pre-existing name-vs-L3 asymmetry on the sessions sink, now visible through the commit-prompt composition). Filed as **Task 206** (fix directions: force-a-roll-before-commit / exclude-now.md-from-the-offered-add / L3-screen-now.md-at-write / gitignore-until-roll).

**Meta — the live gate earned its keep.** Neither the unit suite nor the two-pass code review on the D-303 fix surfaced this; it took a real cold-open Kiro session with an identity echo + the kit's own commit prompt firing to expose the composition. The "live-test every task / the gate finds what unit-green can't" rule, applied to the privacy surface. _Relates Task 148 / ADR-0019 (the screen), ADR-0018 (the commit prompt), the Task-148 review I1 (the now.md→today name-defense this extends), D-303 (the fix whose gate found it), Task 206._

## 2026-07-09 — D-303: BUG + FIX (v0.5.0, tag-blocking) — Kiro IDE auto-extract captured NOTHING the user said; the `USER_PROMPT` hook var arrives empty on Kiro IDE 1.0, so the user turn never reached the transcript and bi-turn extraction starved (P-TQSG9PCA)

**Class:** the "captures automatically" headline was FALSE on Kiro — the exact lazy-framing failure this project forbids, and D-292 (3-agent gate blocks the v0.5.0 tag) exists to catch. The README claims Kiro compatibility; shipping this unfixed would be the lie D-292 guards against.

**Symptom (live, on a clean Kiro IDE folder).** User states a casual preference the model does NOT `mk_remember` ("I always use httpx for HTTP calls, never requests"). On Claude Code this yields a `write_source: auto-extract` fact with no command. On Kiro: nothing durable — every auto-extract run reported `nothing_durable`. Explicit `cmk remember`, recall, and the cross-project cold-open ALL worked; only the *automatic user-preference* path was dead.

**Root cause (confirmed, not guessed — I was wrong FOUR times first by testing throwaway scripts instead of the real function against real data).** Auto-extract's bi-turn extraction needs the USER turn to identify a user-stated preference (an assistant-origin candidate demotes toward discarded). On Kiro IDE, the kit sourced the user turn from `USER_PROMPT` (set by the `userPromptSubmit` hook) → but on **Kiro IDE 1.0 that env var arrives EMPTY** (a Kiro-side regression — their open issues #9619 / #6188 / #7375 / #4620). `capture-prompt.mjs:80` then silently no-ops on the empty string (`{action:'noop', reason:'empty-prompt'}` — the D-269 silent-no-op class), so the user turn never lands in the transcript/now.md, and `captureTurn` at the Stop hook found only an empty `readLastUserTurnFromTranscript`. Assistant-only turns → nothing durable. **Confirmed by:** two independent sonnet research agents (web GitHub issues + our own research base), a local live test, and an end-to-end code trace. Ruled OUT the parser — `parseKiroIdeV1Messages` against a real `~/.kiro` messages.jsonl returns BOTH `userText` and `assistantText` correctly; the reader was never the problem.

**FIX (Option B — make the Stop hook self-sufficient).** `captureTurn` now prefers `payload.user_message` — which the Kiro Stop hook (`kiro-hook-bin.mjs wrappedCapture`) ALREADY populates from `readKiroTurn().userText` (verified working on real IDE `messages.jsonl`) — over the empty-on-Kiro transcript scan. `capture-turn.mjs:415`: `payload.user_message` (Kiro Stop hook) → `readLastUserTurnFromTranscript` (Claude path) fallback. This recovers the prompt from Kiro's OWN reliable session transcript at turn-end, independent of whether `capture-prompt`'s `USER_PROMPT` succeeded. Claude Code + kiro-cli paths unchanged (kiro-cli stores only `user_prompt_length` — user-turn-less by design; unaffected because it never relied on this path for preferences).

**Why Option B over patching `capture-prompt`/`USER_PROMPT`:** the empty env var is a Kiro platform bug we can't fix; the Stop hook already had the data (`readKiroTurn`), so the durable path shouldn't depend on the broken prompt hook AT ALL. Self-sufficiency at the Stop hook is more robust than chasing the platform.

**Self-review caught a privacy gap; code-review caught a data-loss gap (the two-pass discipline earning its keep):**
- **Self-review (PII asymmetry).** `readKiroTurn` returns the user turn RAW (no privacy pass), unlike the transcript fallback which capture-prompt already L1-masked. Without a fix, a Kiro user turn with an email/phone/home-path would land UNMASKED in now.md + the turn-file. Fix: the payload branch now runs the SAME `sanitizePrivacyTags` + (when `screenOn`) `maskPii` + `appendRedactions(source:'capture-turn:user')` the assistant turn does. Transcript fallback is NOT re-masked (already masked at write time).
- **Code-review B1 (transcript-of-record data loss).** The first fix routed the recovered user turn ONLY to now.md + the auto-extract turn-file — NOT to the committed/live transcript (`effectiveTranscriptPath`). On the Claude path capture-prompt writes the `## — user` entry to that transcript at UserPromptSubmit; on Kiro that never fires, so the L3-promoted committed transcript-of-record would silently lose EVERY Kiro user turn (a one-sided dialogue archive, permanent). Fix: when the user turn comes from the payload (Kiro), captureTurn now appends a masked `## ts — user` entry to `effectiveTranscriptPath` BEFORE the assistant entry (correct user→assistant order); on the transcript-fallback path it does NOT double-write (capture-prompt already wrote it — over-mutation guarded by a test asserting exactly one `— user` heading).

**TDD:** failing test (`payload.user_message populates USER_TURN even when the transcript has NO prior user entry`) red → green; plus a masking test (raw email absent + `«EMAIL»` present in all THREE sinks — turn-file, now.md, transcript — and the original in redactions.log under `capture-turn:user`), a transcript-parity assertion (user→assistant order), and a no-double-write over-mutation guard on the Claude path. Full suite **2828/2828, 166 files** green before B1; re-running after B1.

**Verification owed before tag:** live re-test on a fresh clean Kiro folder — casual preference, no `mk_remember`, confirm a `write_source: auto-extract` fact appears (the definitive proof, per the D-169 no-manual-command rule).

## 2026-07-08 — D-302: BUG (filed v0.5.1) — `cmk install` breaks itself on its own running MCP server's DLL lock; documented-never-fixed (P-aLLW62HD; Task 205)

- **The user's catch, mid-repack:** the assistant noted "killing the `cmk mcp serve` procs first — the exact thing that broke the last reinstall," and the user asked *"that is a bug, no?"* — Yes. The kit's own running MCP server holds a Windows lock on `vec0.dll` / `better_sqlite3.node`, so a `npm install -g` reinstall/upgrade can't overwrite them → EBUSY → occasionally a HALF-INSTALLED broken global (`cmk --version` errors). Hit live during v0.5.0 gate-prep (the uninstall completed, the install couldn't).
- **The real bug is documented-never-fixed.** Extensively recorded (`P-SaM22R7B`/`P-EMNaBNWT`, high-trust) but the disposition was always "document + tell the user to kill the server manually" — a workaround, not a fix. A tool that breaks itself when reinstalled while running is a defect; the user shouldn't need to hand-kill processes. Filed as **Task 205, v0.5.1** (per the user's file-immediately rule `P-NNM9F73K`).
- **Not a v0.5.0 tag blocker:** pre-existing, usually cosmetic (the EBUSY typically fires during cleanup and the install still succeeds — verify by `cmk --version` not error count), workaround known, hits reinstall/upgrade not first-install or normal use, doesn't touch the v0.5.0 feature surface.
- **Why HANDLE, not eliminate:** the structural cure (better-sqlite3 → `node:sqlite`) would kill the whole locked-DLL class but is REJECTED on perf (Task 141b, ~10% slower FTS5, fails D-147) — so the DLL fragility is a knowingly-accepted cost, and graceful *handling* (detect-and-stop own server / retry-on-EBUSY / detect-the-half-install-and-say-how-to-recover) is the right layer. _Relates `P-aLLW62HD`, `P-SaM22R7B`/`P-EMNaBNWT`, `P-3VT9YT6L`, `P-VJL254MX`, Task 141b (rejected cure), Task 205, D-301/D-298/D-299 (the sibling v0.5.1 items)._

---

## 2026-07-08 — D-301: FIX (fix-before-v0.5.0-tag) — the L3-promote 20s judge timeout starved the committed transcript on slow-Haiku; site-aware timeout (P-AAHW235S)

- **FOUND on the v0.5.0 cold-open re-test** (cut-gate-coldopen-148b, the FIXED-global run). E2 (the privacy screen) PASSED containment perfectly — but the committed transcript never appeared. Instrumented `promotePendingTranscripts`: `reason = "HaikuViaAnthropicApi: claude --print did not return within 20000ms"`. The L3 judge's `PII_JUDGE_TIMEOUT_MS=20_000` was too tight for the slow-Haiku window that ran all session (the extraction child took 63s, compress 26s — the D-174/D-179 environmental-slowness class). Proven it was PURELY the budget: the same promote at `timeoutMs:90000` succeeded, all 4 pending turns promoted, committed transcript landed SCREENED (`«USER»`, raw username count 0).
- **Two-sided severity (recorded honestly, not lazy-framed):** (a) **fail-closed WORKS** — the timeout → deferred → everything stayed in the gitignored live buffer → nothing unscreened committed (the D-294 invariant held under a real judge timeout, verified live); BUT (b) the happy path is **starved** — on a slow-Haiku day the promote defers indefinitely, so a user (esp. on the cold-open showcase) sees an empty committed transcript and could think capture is broken. Never a leak; a bad-observability + eventual-completeness issue.
- **THE FIX (branch `fix-l3-promote-timeout`, TDD) — site-aware timeout, the D-179/§16.42 pattern applied to the promote:** `PII_JUDGE_TIMEOUT_MS` 20s→**120s** (the ceiling-free DEFAULT for the detached per-turn child — fire-and-forget, no outer hook ceiling, exactly like daily-distill's `CEILING_FREE_TIMEOUT_MS`); NEW `PII_JUDGE_SESSIONEND_TIMEOUT_MS=50s` passed **explicitly** at the SessionEnd top-up (which runs concurrently under the 60s hook ceiling with 50s-capped siblings — the D-92/F-2 two-sided rule: an inner timeout must not exceed the outer ceiling, AND a ceiling-free caller must not inherit a ceiling-sized bound). Plus **observability**: `summarizeSessionEnd` now surfaces the defer REASON (timeout vs reject-gate) — previously invisible, which is why diagnosing this needed live instrumentation.
- **Composition verified** — this is the exact class §16.42 (design line 1509) documents: "the ceiling-free callers get 120s; an inner timeout must not EXCEED an outer hook ceiling (50<60)." The per-file cap `PROMOTE_MAX_FILES_PER_RUN=2` still bounds the SessionEnd worst case to 2×50s, but the concurrent block's wall-clock is max(...) ≈50s < 60s. Budget-pairs registry updated (site-aware).
- **fix-before-tag (the user's call)** — same class + size as D-300: a starved committed transcript on the cold-open showcase is a bad first impression, and the fix is small (a constant bump + one explicit param + the observability line). _Relates P-AAHW235S (the finding), D-179/D-174 (the ceiling-free-timeout class this repeats), design §16.42 (the two-sided composition rule), D-294 (the fail-closed invariant that held), Task 148.8 (PROMOTE_MAX_FILES_PER_RUN — the composition sibling), D-300 (the sibling cold-open fix)._
