## 2026-06-01 — the road to a product: ship-one-wow-at-a-time, video-parity is the done-bar

### Settled decisions (do NOT re-open without new evidence)

- **✅ DECISION (2026-06-29) — D-230: Task 151.3 WIRING = cite-and-sum (the classifier cites source_fact_ids; code sums their recurrence_count to gate). The LLM NEVER counts — 5/5 bridge study unanimous.** D-229 settled THAT recurrence gates (Option B); D-230 settles HOW the LLM-born trait gets its recurrence number. A 6th workflow (wv99dhd5d) code-read the gate→synthesis handoff of the 5 strong-yes systems (MemoryOS/honcho/memclaw/EverOS/captain-claw): **UNANIMOUS — arithmetic counts+selects the unit; the LLM never counts recurrence** (4/5 don't even pass the number into the LLM context). **The asymmetry that made this non-obvious:** their gated unit PRE-EXISTS the LLM (segment/cluster carries its own count); our trait is LLM-BORN (synthesized, no pre-existing row to ORDER BY). **The faithful adaptation (cite-and-sum / B-strict via citation):** the classifier emits `PERSONA CANDIDATE | … | source_fact_ids=[…]` (cites which facts it drew from — NOT a count); code resolves+validates the ids, SUMS their `recurrence_count` (151.1), gates on the sum ≥ 3. **Rejected Option A** (LLM emits recurrence=N) — 5/5 reject LLM-counting; grading a number ≈ grading phrasing (the bug). **Honest caveat (recorded):** the sum is deterministic, but trait MEMBERSHIP is the LLM's grouping choice — acceptable (grouping coherent facts IS synthesis, the LLM's job), NOT the arithmetic-pre-clustering guarantee the 3/5 cluster systems have (we'd prefer that with an embedding-cluster primitive pre-LLM; we lack one, so cite-and-sum is the pragmatic faithful pick). Lands in design.md §20.1 + the [research note ADDENDUM 2](../research/2026-06-29-curation-cluster-code-study.md) + Task 151.3's wiring note. Build state unchanged: 151.1+151.2 committed (53c1bbf, suite 2387/2387 green); 151.3 is the next TDD step (extend PERSONA_CANDIDATE_RE + parsePersonaCandidates for source_fact_ids; add resolveRecurrenceSum; swap the gate at auto-persona.mjs:534). _Relates D-229 (Option B), ADR-0016, design.md §20.1, the bridge study (wv99dhd5d, backed up). The maintainer "research what others did in code before inventing" — the asymmetry was caught by reading the real handoff, not assumed._

- **✅ DECISION (2026-06-29) — D-229: Task 151.3 promotion gate = Option B (recurrence GATES promotion, the LLM only SYNTHESIZES the trait wording), validated by a WIDE 15-system code-read — honest count, NOT inflated.** The maintainer pushed for 5+ real samples, same-shape AND different-shape, and explicitly *"even more projects is better"* + *"it doesn't have to be the same — projects that have a mechanic that deals with this."* Code-read 15 systems on the broad durability-earning question (recurrence vs LLM-judged vs trained-trust vs pure-self-edit vs event-time): MemoryOS, MemOS, memclaw, graphiti, cognee, claude-mem, honcho, memobase, MIRIX, EverOS, TencentDB, letta, langmem, basic-memory, captain-claw. **The honest result (the studies refused to inflate it):** **5 strong-yes parallels to B** (MemoryOS heat≥5.0 / memclaw cluster≥3 / honcho `times_derived` / EverOS cluster≥2 / captain-claw `dream_cycles≥2`) + MemOS partial — **but NONE are our exact shape** (all server/DB/SQLite). **Among systems actually shaped like us** (markdown/git/local — basic-memory, claude-mem, iwe, the awrshift cluster): **ZERO auto-promote by recurrence; our two closest twins have no persona tier at all.** So "5+ similar projects did B" is FALSE; the truth is two-layered: **the mechanism is proven across 5 diverse architectures, but our class hasn't done it — we'd be FIRST.** **Why B is still right for US** (reasons the code revealed, all mapping to our constraints): (1) no per-turn LLM — we're not an observer-LLM architecture, recurrence is free; (2) no ritual (D-169) — recurrence needs no human feedback signal (unlike the `helpful_count`/`report_outcome` the LLM-judged systems need); (3) deterministic/fail-closed/auditable vs an LLM hallucinating confidence on thin evidence; (4) it is the PRECISE fix for the v0.3.1 cold-open bug — a demonstrated-but-not-declared philosophy stranded by a PHRASING gate; recurrence catches it because the evidence was in the recurrence, not the wording. **Honest framing recorded so it's never overclaimed:** NOT "5 similar projects do it" (the peer read kills that); rather "the field's scoring systems converge on recurrence + our peers leave it on the table → we adapt the proven primitive to lead our class, layered on safety they lack (Poison_Guard/conflict-queue/decision-trail)." **The LLM is NOT removed** (the earlier overstatement, corrected mid-discussion): it stays as the SYNTHESIZER of trait wording (MemoryOS pattern: arithmetic heat gates, LLM writes the profile) — what's removed is the LLM from the GATE. **Build status:** 151.1 (recurrence_count field) + 151.2 (computeHeat) shipped on branch `task-151-recurrence-promotion`, full suite 2387/2387 + lint green; 151.3 is the resume point. _Full study → the [curation-cluster research note ADDENDUM](../research/2026-06-29-curation-cluster-code-study.md); the maintainer "even more projects is better" + "it doesn't have to be the same, just a mechanic that deals with it" + "i am not the smartest man… seeing how others did it makes it solid." Relates ADR-0016 (the decision this validates), D-228 (the 7-system design lock), the v0.3.1 cold-open bug (D-177), D-169 (no ritual — why recurrence not LLM-feedback), Task 151.3._

- **📐 DESIGN LOCKED (2026-06-29) — D-228: Task 151 persona-promotion redesign settled by a 7-system CODE-read; folds Task 97; design lives in design.md §20 + ADR-0016 + a research note (NOT a standalone docs/design/ file).** The maintainer asked to "deep research all the version tasks from sources we researched — actual code, then best practice." Ran an 8-agent parallel workflow reading the ACTUAL CODE (not note-summaries) of 7 memory systems — MemoryOS, mem0, letta, langmem, caura-memclaw, graphiti, MemOS — for the curation cluster (promotion 151 / trust 97 / re-curation 95 / loop 179 / skill 180). **The 3 architectural decisions (→ ADR-0016):** (1) **promotion = CAPPED RECURRENCE, not phrasing** — `heat = min(recurrence_count, CAP)·W + lazy exp(-Δh/τ)`, promote at count≥3; the cap is load-bearing (MemOS); NO LLM on the hot path (mem0 ABANDONED its per-fact judge for hash-dedup = evidence it's too costly/unstable). Replaces the form-based `PERSONA_CONFIDENCE_RULE` (fixes Hole A). (2) **trust = a SEPARATE evolving passive-outcome field (folds Task 97)** — `+0.1/−0.15` floor `0.05`, event-driven from contradiction/supersession/restatement (zero ritual, D-169), in the rebuildable INDEX not committed frontmatter (D-218). Two fields not one (the maintainer's call) — recurrence gates promotion, trust gates protection; MemOS proves a unified score lets a noisy fact rank high. (3) **DEMOTE-NOT-EVICT** — the snapshot is a validity-filtered VIEW over a never-deleted store; at cap CONDENSE the file (letta) not fragment-split; sweep drops low-trust-AND-unaccessed first; high-trust persona never swept (fixes Hole B — the cold-open bug). Cross-system consensus: never-delete + status-flip; the MemoryOS-LFU / MemOS-top-N value-blind sweeps ARE the Task-151 bug — don't replicate. **Process notes:** (a) Task 97 ceases to be standalone → ships as Task 151.6–151.8; (b) the design was FIRST written as a standalone `docs/design/` file, which VIOLATES the kit's "design.md is the sole Spine HOW" kernel — the maintainer caught it; corrected to design.md §20 (canonical) + ADR-0016 (decision) + a research note (evidence) — the kit's existing ADR+research link-out pattern, adopted as a working convention because design.md is now 324 KB and should link-out evidence rather than inline it; (c) the standalone was BACKED UP (`C:\cut-gate-backups\`) before removal + content verified copied (the maintainer: "don't just delete docs… I don't want to lose info"). 151 broken into 13 granular sub-tasks (151.1–151.13), each with a Done-when criterion. v0.4.3 = 151 + 70.4 + 74. _The maintainer: "deep research all from sources, actual code, then best practice" + "two fields" + "all of what you said needs its own subtasks" + "backup first, don't delete willy-nilly" + the design.md link-out policy. Relates ADR-0016 (the decision), design.md §20 (the HOW), the [7-system code study](../research/2026-06-29-curation-cluster-code-study.md) (evidence), D-177 (the holes), D-178 (full-redesign-once), D-154 (the down-payment), D-169 (no ritual), D-218 (trust-in-index), Task 151/97/95/66/179/180._

- **📝 REGISTRY PRIOR-ART SWEEP → the space is now CROWDED (positioning update) + 3 sibling projects (2026-06-29) — D-227: a `patent`-tool registry sweep + 3 git-native-markdown-memory siblings (nestwork / pulse8-cortex-vault / nt) → revisit-notes on Tasks 146/176/80/50/148, AND a real positioning correction.** The user wanted to check if anyone's already built our ideas (via the `patent` CLI — a semantic-search over npm/PyPI/GitHub/crates registries). The tool itself is too noisy to adopt (38 fuzzy blurb-matches per query; misses buried-in-code gems — our deep-read is better), but ONE search ("git-native markdown memory for AI agents") delivered the load-bearing finding:
  - **POSITIONING CORRECTION (supersedes the D-224/6-frameworks "the gap is us" note):** the git-native/markdown-memory space is **NO LONGER EMPTY — it's CROWDED.** The sweep surfaced a cluster of direct siblings: **synpulse8/pulse8-ai-cortex** (markdown typed-graph knowledge OS), **songth1ef/nestwork** (git-sync multi-agent memory), **navbytes/nt** (plain-text agent memory), + tomdwipo/claude-soul, codesstar/loci (Memory Palace), clawsouls/soulspec, opencode-graphiti. **So the differentiator is NO LONGER "we're the only markdown/git-native one"** — it's EXECUTION DEPTH (automatic auto-extract capture, trust/Poison_Guard/dedup/conflict-queue, the v0.4 breadth, the cross-project wedge). README/About positioning must shift from "category-novel" to "depth-of-execution" (the 6-frameworks article's "the gap is us" was true weeks ago; the long tail has filled in since).
  - **3 sibling deep-reads → revisit-notes (no new tasks):** **`nestwork`** → Task 146 (PARTITION-BY-WRITER concurrency — each agent owns its dir, writes never collide, no locks; a cheaper answer than lock-the-shared-file) + Task 50 (AGENTS.md-canonical + thin CLAUDE/SOUL/GEMINI mirrors — INDEPENDENT confirmation of our cross-agent shape, handles our exact tail) + Task 148 (a `desensitization contract` — redact-before-commit + optional AI-semantic pass — so 148's "unmatched" claim is corrected: a 10th project does it). **`pulse8-cortex-vault`** (Apache-2.0) → Task 176 read-list: a markdown typed-graph with AUTO-MAINTAINED EDGES on file change + a `vault_context` ranked-subgraph query — the closest reference for "derive/maintain edges from the markdown we already write" (both halves of 176). **`nt`** (MIT) → Task 80: a NEW capture source we lack — a PostToolUse hook mirroring Claude's `TodoWrite` list into memory (we capture turns + edits, NOT the agent's live task-state).
  - **The honest meta-call (the user: "lets write this all up and go back to developing our kit"):** we've thoroughly canvassed the space — each new project is now mostly CONVERGENCE-CONFIRMATION (our architecture is right) + one small idea; marginal value is dropping. Survey winding down; back to kit dev (Task 66/151). _The user: "you want to check them now?" → "lets write this all up and go back to developing our kit." Relates Task 146/50/148 (nestwork), Task 176 (pulse8 typed-graph), Task 80 (nt todo-capture), D-224 (the positioning note this corrects — space went empty→crowded), the patent-tool sweep (discovery instrument, not adopted)._

- **📝 BATCH SURVEY — 2 projects + 8 articles → revisit-notes on recall/graph/fusion tasks (2026-06-29) — D-225 (sift-kg, Elastic) + D-226 (8 MLM articles), with a real PREMISE CORRECTION on Task 178.** Batched survey (the user: "wait with the commit until we finish a couple"). All attached as revisit-notes, no new tasks. **The load-bearing catch:** code-checking during this batch found we **ALREADY use RRF** (`semantic-backend.mjs:314`, the D-72 recipe shipped in Task 65: sqlite-vec + FTS5 bm25 via RRF k=60 + a 0.30/0.40 rerank) — so **Task 178 was mis-framed as "adopt RRF"; corrected to "TUNE k + test adjacent techniques (post-retrieval-filter, HyDE)"** — a smaller task. The articles' value was less the gems than surfacing that correction.
  - **D-225 — `juanceresa/sift-kg`** (MIT, 653★, real code) → Task 176 EDGE-DERIVATION reference: a single combined entity+relation LLM prompt with direction constraints + the smart **schema-free DISCOVER-THEN-FREEZE** mode (LLM designs a schema once, saves `discovered_domain.yaml`, extracts consistently — kills the "every run invents different relations" drift any auto-derived graph faces) + a **3-layer dedup ladder** (deterministic→LLM-propose→decide) that CONFIRMS our conflict-queue propose-don't-merge posture. Catch: document-corpus + structural-recall + human-merge — the edge-CONSTRUCTION reference, not the graph-QUERY one.
  - **D-225 — Elastic hybrid-search explainer** → Task 178 fusion-science prior: RRF (`Σ1/(k+rank)`) vs linear/weighted, and the WHY — BM25 unbounded + cosine fixed-range means a naive weighted-sum (captain-claw) biases toward bigger-absolute-scores unless normalized; RRF sidesteps it. So 178's benchmark carries a HYPOTHESIS (RRF ≥ weighted-avg) not a blind sweep. Covers only fusion, no rerank.
  - **D-226 — 8 MachineLearningMastery articles** (mostly 101-level confirmation; the real gems): **(→178)** k=60 standard RRF constant, retrieve-ALL-before-fuse (avoid truncation bias), **post-retrieval filtering** (re-score candidates vs the live query, drop low scorers before injecting — we lack this), **HyDE/query-expansion** (embed a hypothetical answer — a paraphrase-recall lever for Task 99); **(→176/95)** **RAPTOR** (recursive cluster→summarize→cluster the summaries — a memory summary-HIERARCHY for big-picture recall; the one genuinely-new idea) + the GraphRAG/Leiden community-summary mechanism + Cognee flagged as the priority clone; **(→149)** "memory as a TOOL the agent chooses to call" is a NAMED recognized pattern — external validation our judgment-pulled `memory-search` skill is a design, NOT a workaround; **(→74)** static-vs-dynamic two-pass context (= our frozen snapshot, confirmed) + "anchored iterative summarization" (preserve decisions/state when compressing) + "Lost in the Middle" (attention favors start+end → injection order matters); **(metadata)** filter-BEFORE-scoring — our keyword path already does (`search.mjs:322` SQL WHERE before rank), verify the vec KNN path does too.
  - **POSITIONING (a D-224 sibling):** the "6 best memory frameworks 2026" article lists mem0/zep/langchain/llamaindex/letta/cognee — ALL vector/graph/SQL, **NONE file-based/markdown/git-native**, and explicitly says *"this gap suggests a competitive opportunity if such a tool doesn't yet dominate."* **That gap is the kit.** README/About positioning validation: we're the local-first/markdown/git-native answer no major framework occupies. _The user: "fishing for gems / write everything down / commit the batch." Relates Task 176 (sift-kg edge-derivation + RAPTOR + Cognee), Task 178 (RRF premise correction + Elastic science + post-filter/HyDE), Task 149 (memory-as-tool validation), Task 74 (context-engineering framings), Task 65 (the shipped RRF the correction found), D-224 (the positioning sibling), D-217/D-221 (the prior fusion notes this corrects/extends)._

- **📝 PITCH-COPY NOTE (2026-06-29) — D-224: a popular ML article independently states the kit's exact thesis ("context windows are not memory"); two framings are README/About copy candidates — NO task (it's positioning, not work).** The MachineLearningMastery article *"Context Windows Are Not Memory"* (<https://machinelearningmastery.com/context-windows-are-not-memory-what-ai-agent-developers-need-to-understand/>) is mechanically 101-level — every pattern it names (retrieval / compression / summarization / query-at-turn-start-commit-at-end) the kit already does, more rigorously; **no tech gem, no task.** But it's external VALIDATION of the kit's reason-to-exist (the model is stateless — "every API call starts at step zero" — and a bigger context window does NOT fix cross-session amnesia; durable memory does), and two of its framings are strong PITCH COPY for where the kit is weaker than its engineering (the README/About/messaging):
  - **"Stop trying to buy a 10-million-token desk. Get a normal desk, teach it how to open the filing cabinet."** — near-perfect metaphor for the kit: the injected snapshot is the desk (bounded, always-on), `cmk search` is opening the filing cabinet (the deeper archive). The user 2026-06-29: *"that's a good line."* Candidate for the README opener / About.
  - **"Agents should act as database administrators, not databases"** — sharp one-line framing of recall (the agent QUERIES memory, it isn't memory) = our Task 75 recall-trigger/authority-preamble thesis in one phrase.
  - **Use:** when we next polish the README "what it does" / GitHub About copy, these two lines are ready-made; could even cite the article as third-party validation of the context≠memory thesis. NOT a tasks.md item (positioning, not a deliverable) — logged here so the copy isn't lost. _The user shared it as "a nice article" + "that's a good line." Relates the README capability-surface rule (where this copy would land), Task 75 (the DB-admin framing = recall-trigger), the kit's founding thesis (context≠memory = why the kit exists)._

- **📝 MEMCLAW REVISIT → CODE-DIVED; the "Forge" unblocks 3 tasks (2026-06-29) — D-223: caura-memclaw shipped a WORKING memory→skill-synthesis pipeline + passive-outcome-signal inference since our 2026-06-12 README-only look — gems attached to Tasks 180/177/97/95/75.0, no new task.** The user re-shared memclaw (article + repo) "even if we saw it, good to revisit" — and it had UPDATED (last commit 2026-06-29). Our own 2026-06-12 note said "code-dive FIRST before citing"; did it now. Two production subsystems are NEW since:
  - **THE FORGE / "Skill Factory" = a real memory→skill-synthesis pipeline (was vaporware at our README review; now `forge_service.py`+`distill_prompt.py`+`fingerprint.py`+spec).** This is the working version of **Task 180** (which we'd filed as exploratory/"maybe too far"). Pipeline: build session traces → infer outcome PASSIVELY → cluster repeated workflows → gate on volume+diversity → LLM-distill a candidate `SKILL.md` with provenance (`cites`/`evidence`/`fingerprint`) → fingerprint-dedup + rejected-cooloff → NEVER auto-promote (earn-it gates). UPGRADES 180's GO/NO-GO from "is it possible?" (proven) to "does a single user generate enough recurrence to clear the gate, or does the persona already cover it?" Catch: memclaw is Postgres+multi-tenant+FLEET — we adapt the SHAPE (recurrence-across-sessions, not across-agents), not the server machinery.
  - **THE KEY UNBLOCK — 6 PASSIVE outcome signals crack the "signal without a user ritual" gap that had BOTH Task 177 (train-the-doc) and Task 97 (dynamic trust) stuck.** Both needed a success/failure signal for an LLM-graded / outcome-scored update, but asking the user violates D-169, and pro-workflow/caura's earlier patterns assumed an explicit signal we reject. memclaw's `session_trace.py` infers outcome PASSIVELY: contradiction-detected / fact-superseded / repeat-recall / terminal-memory-classification / cross-session-reuse / git-CI. **We already produce signals 1/2/5** (contradiction-queue, `superseded_by`, index access tracking) → a passive confidence/validation signal is reachable with NO prompt. This is the same primitive 177's validation-gate AND 97's trust-delta both need — it makes both BUILDABLE instead of "needs-design, open." The single most valuable find of the survey thread.
  - **Smaller notes:** fingerprint-dedup-of-rejected (`sha256(goal+domain+entities+steps)` + cooloff TTL) → Task 95 (don't re-propose a declined merge); keystones (governance-as-data, real prompt-position-+-framing override, confirmed in `context-engine.ts`) → Task 75.0 INDEPENDENT VALIDATION of our authority preamble (their scope-merge/trust-gating = fleet-only, skip; the "append-last-for-recency" placement is a data-point if the preamble ever gets ignored mid-long-context).
  - **Per the standing rule:** revisit-notes on the tasks that will USE these, NOT new tasks (the user: "attach as notes so we revisit how it symbiotes with our architecture"). The 2026-06-12 memclaw research note's caveat ("README-level — code-dive before citing") is now DISCHARGED for the Forge/keystones/passive-signal claims (code-verified). _The user: "we saw this before but good to revisit" + "go over the github again, it updated." Relates Task 180 (Forge=the reference), Task 177+97 (the passive-signal unblock — the big one), Task 95 (fingerprint-dedup), Task 75.0 (keystones validation), D-218/D-221 (the 97/180 prior notes this builds on), D-169 (why passive-not-prompted is the unblock), the 2026-06-12 memclaw note (caveat discharged), the read-past-the-README + code-dive-before-citing disciplines._

- **📝 SURVEY NEAR-DEAD-END + an ANTI-PATTERN to NOT re-propose (2026-06-28) — D-222: `amanaiproduct/personal-os` — its headline "ask-to-clarify before capturing" VIOLATES the kit's no-prompt thesis (rejected); its one thin keeper is a manual-end contrast for Task 177.** personal-os (512★, CC BY-NC-SA non-commercial) is a task-manager workflow wrapper (brain-dump → prioritized tasks), file-based, no real memory layer — off-topic. Dug past the README into `core/mcp/server.py` + `core/evals/` (the gem-hunt discipline). Two findings:
  - **(REJECTED — record so it's not re-proposed) ambiguity-detection → ask-clarifying-questions before storing.** `is_ambiguous()` + `generate_clarification_questions()` detect a vague item ("fix bug", "follow up") and ASK the user to clarify before committing it. **This is an ANTI-PATTERN for the kit (the user 2026-06-28: "ask before capturing is not good, it goes against the kit's all point").** Prompting the user before a capture IS the manual ritual D-85/D-169 exist to kill — the kit NEVER asks; memory just works. The kit's answer to a low-quality candidate is the SILENT trust-routing we already ship (auto-demote/skip, no prompt), NOT a clarifying question. Nothing to take; recorded so a future session doesn't mistake "ask-to-clarify" for a gem.
  - **(thin keeper) session-eval → improve AGENTS.md = the MANUAL-end contrast for Task 177.** `core/evals/trace_to_eval.py` reads Claude Code's saved sessions (`~/.claude/projects/`), generates an eval (intent / tool-summary / AI-suggested judgement+patterns), the user annotates it, then "apply suggestions to AGENTS.md." It's a human-in-the-loop, manual version of correction-trained-doc (Task 177) — the SIMPLE end of the spectrum vs pro-workflow's automated DSPy optimizer (the complex end). Useful only as a contrast data-point when 177 is designed; NOT adopted (manual = against D-169). Also: their dedup is `SequenceMatcher` + keyword-Jaccard (0.7/0.3) = the SAME lexical approach as our `detectConflicts`, and we're ahead (we add semantic dedup) — no gem.
  - **Net: a near-dead-end** (1 anti-pattern rejected, 1 thin manual-contrast for 177). No task, no task-note beyond what 177 already carries. The valuable record here is the REJECTION (ask-before-capture is off-thesis) + the gem-hunt confirmation that both findings were ONLY in the code, never the README. _The user: "I'm fishing for gems / dig for gold" + "ask before capturing goes against the kit's all point." Relates D-85/D-169 (the no-prompt thesis ask-to-clarify violates), Task 177 (the manual-contrast), the read-past-the-README discipline._

- **📝 SURVEY FIND → 4 REVISIT-NOTES, no new task (2026-06-28) — D-221: `kstevica/captain-claw`'s memory layer (MIT, in-repo, readable) is the best insight-generation + dynamic-trust reference found — gems attached as "revisit when building" notes to Tasks 179/97/178/66, not a 7th task.** captain-claw (96★, MIT) is an agent-orchestration platform (off-topic) but its MEMORY subsystem is real (5,337 LOC) and READABLE — unlike taOS (unpublished engine) or Anthropic Dreams (closed product). Deep-read of `nervous_system.py`/`insights.py`/`semantic_memory.py`/`deep_memory.py`. **Three gems + one novel idea, each attached to the task it informs (so they're found at build time, not lost):**
  - **→ Task 179 (insight loop):** captain-claw's `nervous_system.py` "dream" pass is the READABLE reference for the insight half (vs closed Dreams) — every 12 messages an LLM reads across memory layers + open contradictions → ≤3 typed **intuitions**, FTS5-deduped, injected ranked by `confidence×importance`. Steal the prompt shape + the `raw`→`maturing`→`mature` maturation gate + `source_ids` provenance. AVOID their weakness: maturation is time-gated not evidence-gated.
  - **→ Task 97 (dynamic trust):** their `validate()` (+0.2 conf on confirm) / `record_access()` / `run_decay()` (0.05/day after 7d unaccessed, validated-never-decays, cap 500) is working evolving-trust — stronger than the caura/memory-os summaries. Confirms the SHAPE but has the SAME open gap (no automatic outcome-feedback; confidence only moves on manual validate) — so it doesn't solve the "where's the success signal without a ritual" problem either.
  - **→ Task 178 (fusion):** a THIRD fusion to benchmark — weighted-average (`0.65·vector + 0.35·keyword`) + 21-day exponential temporal decay on the score. So 178's candidate set = weighted-avg vs RRF vs ours vs cross-encoder.
  - **→ Task 66 (the NOVEL idea — "tension-holding"):** captain-claw's `unresolved` intuition type deliberately does NOT resolve a contradiction — it HOLDS it as a first-class open question across dream cycles (decaying half-speed), resolving later with a lifespan metric. EVERY other system (incl. ours) is additive/latest-wins. Genuinely new. **But it cuts against our "clean committed files" + D-169 "memory just works" model** (a deliberately-unresolved loose end in git-committed `context/`), so it's a REVISIT note on 66.4: decide at build time whether "hold the tension" fits (maybe as a `status: open-question` fact the digest surfaces) or whether latest-wins is right for a memory KIT (vs an exploratory agent). **Why notes not a task (the user's call):** *"attach it to tasks as a note so when we get there we know to revisit and see how these ideas symbiote with our kit's code + architecture"* — enrich the tasks that will USE these, don't proliferate survey tasks. The symbiosis-with-our-architecture question is answered at build time, with the reference in hand. _Relates Task 179/97/178/66 (the enriched tasks), D-219 (the loop/fusion tasks these inform), D-169 (the clean-files thesis tension-holding must reconcile with), the read-past-the-README discipline (captain-claw's README sells orchestration; the memory layer is the buried value)._

- **📝 SURVEY DEAD-END (2026-06-28) — D-220: `jaylfc/taOS` surveyed → NO task (the borrowable part is unreachable + restrictively licensed; it only CONFIRMS a direction we've already laned).** taOS (514★) is a self-hosted agent OS (web desktop, 36 apps, distributed compute, multi-channel hub) — off-topic; we don't build that. Its memory subsystem **`taosmd`** claims our EXACT roadmap (temporal knowledge graph + validity windows + contradiction detection + hybrid BM25/vector + cross-encoder rerank, "97.0% on LongMemEval-S") — but the **engine is NOT in the repo**: the repo only ships an `httpx` CLIENT that calls `taosmd` as an external service on `:7900` (`POST /ingest`, `GET /search?mode=bm25` — `routes/user_memory.py`); the implementation is unpublished. Plus the license is **"Sustainable Use License — source-available, not open source"** (commercial license to sell), murkier to borrow from than the MIT/Apache projects. **Net: no new task** — there's nothing to deep-read and nothing to build that Tasks 66 (temporal validity) / 176 (graph-recall) / 178 (hybrid+rerank) don't already cover. **What it's worth:** (1) independent VALIDATION that a 514★ project converged on the exact temporal+graph+rerank stack we laned (confidence signal, not new idea); (2) its 97% LongMemEval-S claim is a BAR worth noting for Task 99's benchmark (unverifiable — no engine to run). Recorded so the survey trail shows WHY we looked and found nothing actionable. _The user: "this is not helpful." Relates Task 66/176/178 (the laned stack it validates), Task 99 (the benchmark bar), the survey thread (links 1-3 productive: awrshift→174/175, iwe→176, pro-workflow→177/178; link 4 taOS→dead-end)._

- **📝 3 RESEARCH TASKS from the survey follow-ups (2026-06-28) — D-219: memory-linking is the START of a graph (we already write edges, never traverse them → Task 176 narrowed); RRF/hybrid-search-tuning filed so it won't get lost (Task 178); the unified memory-improvement loop filed grounded in a Hermes deep-read (Task 179).** Three follow-ups from the user reviewing the survey borrows:
  - **(176 narrowed) "how do we link memories? isn't this the start of a graph?"** — YES. Code check confirmed the kit ALREADY writes two edge types: **`links` → `related:` frontmatter / `[[cross-links]]`** (`remember-core.mjs:72`, `mcp-server.mjs:642`, `write-fact.mjs:116`) + **`superseded_by`** as a real fact→fact FK (`index-db.mjs:72` `REFERENCES observations(id)`). They're WRITTEN but DEAD — nothing traverses them (no BFS, no "connected facts", no chain query). So the graph-recall task (176) NARROWS from "derive edges from scratch" (the iwe-framed version) to "ACTIVATE the edges we already have" — a much cheaper start. The user was right: it's the start of a graph.
  - **(178 filed) RRF/hybrid-search-tuning → a TASK, not a logged note.** The user: *"we don't want to research a hybrid-search-tuning task? we should make it a task so it won't get lost."* Correct — a "logged note" is how things get lost. Filed Task 178: evaluate `pro-workflow`'s Reciprocal Rank Fusion (BM25+vector, k=60) + other fusion/rerank methods against the kit's hybrid (Task 65), measured ON the Task 99 benchmark (the same harness that decided 65 — numbers decide, D-109 discipline). Research-only; an ADOPT is a small swap.
  - **(179 filed) the memory-improvement LOOP → the umbrella research task, grounded in a Hermes deep-read.** The user: *"a memory-loop feature to make skills/memory better, surface insight — I think Hermes does this; go look, make it a task."* Read Hermes (`insights.py`/`curator.py`/`darwinian-evolver`). **HONEST finding (read-past-the-README): Hermes's "insights" are PURE STATISTICS** (token/session/cost counts, no LLM, no pattern discovery) — NOT the insight-generation the name implies; and its curator never feeds back into the agent's prompt (no closed learning loop). What Hermes DOES do cleanly is **flavor C: skill-library curation** — inactivity-gated archive-stale + an optional LLM "merge overlapping skills into umbrella skills" pass, with a notable **3-layer consolidation classifier** (`absorbed_into` > heuristic tool-call audit > YAML block) so scheduled jobs don't break across merges. **THE KIT HAS NO skill-curation analogue** (we scaffold skills, never prune/merge accumulated ones) — a genuinely new angle. The cross-project read showed the field does memory-improvement in FOUR flavors: (A) re-curate facts [mem0/letta/Dreams ↔ our Task 95], (B) generate insight [Dreams ↔ our 55], (C) curate the skill library [Hermes — NEW for us], (D) train the behavior doc [pro-workflow ↔ our 177]. **Task 179 is the UMBRELLA** that decides unified-loop-vs-separate-features and maps 95/55/177 + the new flavor-C angle onto one architecture — run FIRST in the curation lane so it frames the others. _The user: "how do we link memories", "make RRF a task so it won't get lost", "I think Hermes does this — go look, make it a task". Relates Task 176 (graph-recall, narrowed), Task 178 (RRF, new), Task 179 (the loop, new), Task 95/55/177 (the flavors it unifies), the read-past-the-README discipline (Hermes "insights"=stats), D-217 (the pro-workflow + RRF source), D-216 (the iwe graph-recall source)._
  - **FOLLOW-UP CORRECTIONS (same day, the user's two catches):** **(a) Task 179 de-muddled — DROPPED the "skill-library curation" flavor.** The Hermes deep-read had me add "curate the skill library" as a flavor of the memory-loop, but the user caught it: *"what skill are we improving? we only have memory-write/search."* Correct — our 2 skills are FIXED infrastructure; we have no user-accumulated skill library to prune/merge (that's Hermes's model, not ours). 179 corrected: the loop is re-curate FACTS (95) + surface INSIGHT (55) + improve the PERSONA from corrections (177/151) — memory + persona, not skills. **(b) Task 180 NEW (exploratory) — memory→skill SYNTHESIS.** The user's adjacent idea: *"create a skill BASED ON how the user works + instructions + memories, then a loop that improves that skill… maybe I'm going too far… maybe a different task."* It IS a different task — DISTINCT from 151 (which injects facts into the snapshot) because it would EMIT an artifact (a generated `SKILL.md`). Filed separately + flagged EXPLORATORY (first job = a GO/NO-GO: is this real, or does the injected persona already deliver it?), kept OUT of 179 so the loop stays clean. The user's self-aware "maybe a different task" was right. **(c) Task 176 graph-research scope EXPANDED.** The user: *"there are a lot of projects we researched that do the graph thing — a more thorough research."* Confirmed: our base names 7+ graph systems (mem0 132× / graphiti 88× / zep 59× / memos 41× / **cognee 30× — cited but never deep-read** / memoryos 19× / neo4j). 176's survey clause rewritten to require CLONING + reading the actual code of all relevant ones → a dedicated graph-memory research note feeding the ADR. The thorough read happens at task-time; the scope is locked now so it isn't thin. _Relates Task 179 (corrected), Task 180 (new, exploratory), Task 176 (graph scope expanded), Task 151/177 (the persona-vs-skill distinction), the read-past-the-README discipline (Hermes skill-curation doesn't fit us)._

- **🗂️ BACKLOG DEEP-AUDIT (2026-06-28) — D-218: a 13-task primary-source investigation re-laned the stale-versioned backlog — 2 folds, 1 merge, 1 reframe, 1 verify-and-shrink, 1 version pinned, the rest backlog-with-honest-status.** The user: *"take all the tasks we have open and pin them to an actual version"* → then sharpened: *"do an actual research on all of them — see if we shipped it, see if we took it from a project, see how they did it, decide which task / which version"* → *"clone every project and actually go deep on each."* Ran a parallel workflow: 13 investigators, each reading OUR shipped code + the ACTUAL source code of the project(s) each task draws from (graphiti / OpenHands / mem0 / letta / caura-memclaw / basic-memory / hermes / claude-mem, cloned at `C:\tmp\deep-survey\`). **The discipline win:** the research notes are SUMMARIES; reading the real code corrected several framings (71's "refuse" is wrong; 96's "recovery" is ~80% free from git; 74 already works). **Outcomes:**
  - **Task 66 → v0.4.4 (the ONLY hard-pinned).** The temporal-validity engine (facts stay true as they age) = the wedge "wow #3" currency feature. Gem: graphiti `resolve_edge_contradictions` (close old window `invalid_at=new.valid_at`, NEVER delete → `as_of` falls out free; ONE LLM pass classifies dedup+contradiction together; anchor validity to the episode's `reference_time` not wall-clock). Ship 66.1+66.3 (no-LLM) first; 66.2/66.4 gate on the detection design.
  - **Task 59 → FOLDED into Task 66.** "Reconcile contradictions" IS graphiti's invalidation arithmetic pointed at decision-facts = the same engine 66 builds. D-166's "66 before 95" extended. 59's digest-flag deliverable → 66.4 + the shipped decisions-journal.
  - **Task 130 → MERGED into Task 96; Task 96 REFRAMED + re-laned v0.5.** Primary-source read showed "point-in-time recovery" is ~80% ALREADY SHIPPED via git (`git show <sha>:context/…` + archive/superseded + tombstones; letta + basic-memory both lean on git). The genuinely-new/hard half is COMPLIANCE SCRUB (redact a secret from ALL history) — which git RESISTS (force-push breaks clones, fights the portable-clone niche D-27) and NOBODY solves cleanly (mem0 = all-or-nothing `DROP TABLE`; letta = coarse CASCADE). 96 reframed to lead with the scrub; 130 (`cmk purge --hard`) absorbed as its verb; re-laned off shipped-past v0.3.x → v0.5 (a compliance feature, not a core-loop wow). ADR-first on the git-rewrite tension.
  - **Task 71 → REFRAMED (refuse → reconcile+flag).** The original "detect + REFUSE hand-edits" (modeled on Hermes) FIGHTS our git-native wedge (we WANT committed/PR-able/diff-able memory). basic-memory — the most git-native competitor — RECONCILES external edits (mtime/checksum → re-index, never reject), identical to our markdown-as-truth + rebuildable-SQLite model. Reframed: detect drift (provenance-sha mismatch — the primitive exists, unwired) → re-screen through Poison_Guard + FLAG (doctor HC + optional pre-commit), the win is the COMMIT boundary, not write-refusal. Nice-to-have safety feature, NOT a bug.

Shrinks from a feature-build to: a no-setup test pinning the post-compact snapshot + fix stale ADR-0006 + LIVE-confirm. Folds into the inject-hardening lane (patch-shaped). Caveat: verified vs the hooks DOC, not a live compaction.

Hermes is the LONE primary source (mem0/letta/MemoryOS don't defend stored memory pre-inject — verified). Confirmed our gap: `checkPoisonGuard` is write-time only; `inject-context` never imports it.

- **📝 NOTE + RESEARCH TASK + SEARCH-NOTE (2026-06-28) — D-217: surveying `rohitg00/pro-workflow` (the most credible competitor yet) — we're AHEAD on the core memory loop, they're ahead on ONE thing we don't attempt (training the behavior doc from corrections → Task 177); RRF-search noted, not tasked.** `pro-workflow` (npm, TypeScript, better-sqlite3+FTS5, 34 skills / 37 hooks / 24 events, Claude Code + Cursor + 32 agents via SkillKit) is the closest thing to the kit surveyed so far — same pitch ("gets smarter every session"), same stack. **The read-past-the-README payoff (decisive, code-verified):** its headline "corrections auto-loaded on session start" is **terminal-only** — `session-start.js` writes every learning via `console.error` (stderr) and **NO hook anywhere emits `additionalContext`/`hookSpecificOutput`** (the documented CC context-injection mechanism). So the model NEVER sees the memory unless the human reads the console + re-types it. **Our frozen-snapshot SessionStart injection actually puts memory in the model's context** — they show it to the human. **Two places we're clearly ahead:** (1) recall INJECTION (above — the load-bearing gap); (2) capture is regex-detect + manual `[LEARN]` markup (the user must tag corrections), where ours auto-extracts each turn (D-85/D-169). Plus the maturity gaps: no dedup (same correction = 2 rows), no trust/decay, no contradiction handling, no privacy screen on learnings, no pruning. **Where THEY'RE genuinely ahead (the buried gem the README never mentions):** a **DSPy-style offline skill OPTIMIZER** (`src/optimizer/`) — treats `SKILL.md` as trainable text-space state, corrections as training trajectories, holds out 20% as a validation set, Reflect→Aggregate→Clip→Apply→Evaluate (LLM-graded, accept-on-improvement, budget-capped, periodic consolidation rewrite). It TRAINS the behavior doc; we store FACTS. That's a real bet we don't make → **filed as Task 177** (research+ADR; sibling to Task 55 behavioral-patterns + 151 promotion — a correction may be a signal to UPDATE a rule, validated, not just a fact to store). **Also notable (not borrowed):** their auto-research wiki loop (seed-BFS → fetch → summarize → grow overnight) = OFF our thesis (build topic KBs, not memory-of-you — same skip as awrshift's `/close-day`); and their **Reciprocal Rank Fusion** (BM25+vector, k=60) **possibly cleaner than our hybrid** (Task 65). **RRF decision (the user's call):** do NOT breadcrumb it onto the SHIPPED Task 65/99 (`[x]` tasks — dead weight); log it as a data-point + a note in the recall lane, and evaluate it against the Task 99 benchmark IF/WHEN a v0.4 hybrid-search-tuning task opens. Numbers decide, not vibes. _The user: "add research task" (the optimizer) + "breadcrumb on tasks we already did? v0.3.x already shipped, we're at v0.4.2" (the RRF correction — right, no breadcrumb on closed tasks). Relates Task 177 (the optimizer research task), Task 55/151/97 (the curation siblings), Task 65/99 (the shipped hybrid RRF would tune), the read-past-the-README discipline (their stderr-not-inject gap is the textbook case), D-85/D-169 (auto-capture vs their manual [LEARN] markup)._

- **📝 NOTE + RESEARCH TASK (2026-06-28) — D-216: surveying `iwe-org/iwe` surfaced the graph-recall question — should the kit add a relational/graph query mode beside keyword + semantic? Filed as Task 176 (research + ADR, NOT commit-to-build).** `iwe` (Rust markdown knowledge-graph + LSP + MCP, 1.2k★, Apache-2.0) bills itself as a "markdown memory system" but the CODE tells the real story (the read-past-the-README lesson): **it has ZERO auto-capture** — the human authors every note; the AI only queries/refactors. So it's a different PRODUCT (developer PKM, not agent memory) and we're NOT behind it on memory (it isn't doing memory). **Its buried gem (README undersells it):** link-integrity-preserving structural refactoring — a real `Graph` + `RefIndex` edge map so renaming a doc auto-updates all 1000 references, extract/inline are transactional at the graph level, and the same core serves LSP+CLI+MCP. That gem doesn't transfer (it solves hand-authored-note-web problems our auto-capture model means our users don't have). **What DOES transfer is the QUESTION:** `iwe` makes graph traversal a first-class recall mode (`$includes`/`$referencedBy` bounded-depth BFS) — and we answer EVERYTHING with flat similarity (FTS5 + semantic). **Confirmed no existing task asks this:** graphiti/Zep graph-memory is in our research base (2026-06-06 deep-dive; Task 66 borrowed their bi-temporal model) but we only mined those for TEMPORAL + PROMOTION lessons, never "do we want a graph QUERY layer." Task 149 asks WHEN to recall; this asks WHAT recall MODES — complementary, not duplicate. **Decision: file the research task, don't commit to building.** Adding a graph layer is a real architectural bet — and the HARD CONSTRAINT is that any edges must be AUTO-DERIVED from the markdown we already write (fact↔fact, decision↔file, `[[wikilink]]`-style), rebuildable like the FTS5/vec index — NOT a hand-authored graph (the `iwe` model presupposes human-authored links auto-capture means we don't have). The ADR decides ADOPT (+derive-edges design +lane) / DEFER / REJECT. _The user: "do we have a task to research this? if not lets add it, maybe it's a good fit to add graph recall." Relates Task 176 (the new research task), Task 149 (WHEN-to-recall sibling), Task 65 (semantic), Task 66 (the bi-temporal borrow from the same graph systems), the read-past-the-README discipline, the markdown-native + derive-don't-hand-author constraint._

- **📝 NOTE + 3 BORROWS (2026-06-28) — D-215: a same-named GitHub project (`awrshift/claude-memory-kit`) exists; no technical collision, and 3 of its ideas are worth taking (Tasks 174, 175, + a 151 enrichment).** The user found `https://github.com/awrshift/claude-memory-kit` — same repo name, **different product** (Python/Shell, NOT on npm, freelancer/agency framing, a MANUAL `/close-day` ritual, 23★, MIT). **Collision assessment: the ONLY overlap is the bare repo name** — there is NO technical collision: our npm package is scoped (`@lh8ppl/claude-memory-kit`, globally unique; they don't publish), our CLI is `cmk` (theirs is a `/close-day` slash command), different language, different install (`npm i` vs `git clone`). No trademark issue (both MIT; "claude-memory-kit" is descriptive). **Rename: NOT now, but the trigger has FIRED.** ADR-0012 (SETTLED 2026-05-29) already defers the permanent cross-agent product name to "when cross-agent support materializes" — and v0.4 (Kiro) is that moment, so the rename is now *ripe*, not hypothetical. The `awrshift` collision is extra reason to take the umbrella name (ADR-0012 floats `agent-memory-kit`/`mnemo`/`recall`) at the next deliberate rebrand milestone — NOT mid-patch. The user: *"we are already going to change the name once the kit can work on more than just Claude… we wrote it in the plan."* Correct — it's ADR-0012. **3 borrows (all laned):** **(174, v0.4.x)** git-history backfill — reconstruct a working-day's session log from commits when no `sessions/` entry was written (a real timeline-hole we lack; automatic on the daily-distill cron per D-169). **(175, v0.4.x, depends Task 50)** `cmk tour` — narrate the user's OWN tiers (warm onboarding; their `/tour` is the one UX idea worth taking — we're strong on automation, weak on onboarding warmth; needs the cross-agent seam for the per-agent slash surface). **(151 enrichment, v0.4.3)** their propose-then-confirm promotion UX — the recurrence-scoring half CONFIRMS 151's already-decided FREQUENCY+RECURRENCE direction (not new); the new bit is an OPTIONAL in-conversation "I promoted X" mention (heads-up, NOT a gate — keeps our silent auto-drain default; asking-first would re-introduce the human-in-the-loop D-169 removed). **NOT borrowed:** their manual `/close-day` ritual (our D-169 anti-pattern — the cautionary contrast) and their time-based crystallization (we model it more rigorously via tiers+trust). _The user: "go over all of their docs and code… maybe we can steal something" + "task and version lane them." Relates ADR-0012 (the rename plan — trigger fired), Task 174/175 (the new tasks), Task 151 (the enrichment), D-169 (no-manual-ritual — why their /close-day is the contrast + why the 151 mention is a heads-up not a gate), the RELEASE-PLAN v0.4.x lane._

- **🔒 SECURITY PATCH (2026-06-28) — D-214: fixed the 3 open CodeQL alerts; shipped as v0.4.2, sliding Task 151 → v0.4.3.** Post-v0.4.1, GitHub code-scanning had 3 open alerts on `main`: **#29 [Medium] `js/prototype-pollution-utility`** (`config-core.mjs setDeep` walks a user-controlled dotted key without guarding `__proto__`/`constructor`/`prototype`) and **#21+#24 [High] `js/regex-injection` + `js/incomplete-sanitization`** (`changelog-release.mjs extractReleaseNotes` interpolates `version` into a RegExp escaping only `.`). **Neither was live-exploitable** — the public config API already guards via `hasForbiddenSegment` (with a test), and `version` is `parseSemver`-validated to `^\d+\.\d+\.\d+$` — but CodeQL analyzes each function in isolation and both were technically un-hardened. **Fixed REAL, not dismissed:** `setDeep` self-guards (reusing `hasForbiddenSegment` so the set can't drift) + escapes-every-metacharacter `escapeRegExp` (canonical MDN). **The load-bearing process moment (the user):** my first pass left the new `setDeep` throw branch (+ a pre-existing `configSet` catch line) UNTESTED, and I framed it as "acceptable unreachable defense-in-depth." The user pushed back — *"I don't like that we have a bug and we don't fix it. unless we have a real reason not to fix it."* — which correctly named the lazy-framing: an untested defensive branch IS a gap, and "it's unreachable" wasn't a real reason (it also risked the coverage ratchet). Fix: exported `setDeep` (a legitimate security-invariant boundary) + added a direct guard test AND a catch-path test → `config-core.mjs` 100% lines. **Versioning (the patch-digit rule):** a security fix has no differentiator → it's a patch → takes the next number (v0.4.2), and Task 151 (persona) slides to v0.4.3. Per D-157 ("the patch digit tracks shipments, not pre-numbered; order may shift on real signal"), this is anticipated, not a re-lane conflict — a ready security fix jumps ahead of a larger in-flight feature. Suite 2382/2382 + stress; two-pass reviewed (the skill pass confirmed escapeRegExp's metachar set is complete + that configSet is setDeep's only caller, wrapped in try/catch). _Relates the config-core prototype guard (the original config task's skill-review), security.yml (CodeQL), D-157 (the patch-digit rule), the lazy-framing rule (the user's catch). The 3 alerts re-scan + auto-close on `main` after merge._

- **✅ FIX + RESOLUTION of D-212 (2026-06-27) — D-213: prompt-free capture is delivered by a `PermissionRequest` HOOK that auto-approves the kit's own tools/skills — NOT by allow-list rules (Task 172).** This RESOLVES the D-212 open investigation. **Verdict on cause:** the capture popups are a **Claude Code 2.1.x change, not a kit regression** — git shows the skill + allow-list config byte-stable since Task 108/117; it started prompting the day CC updated. **External proof (the user found it):** anthropics/claude-code **#17499** (auto-approving MCP tools via a skill's `allowed-tools` is undocumented + unreliable — another developer hit the identical "still asks for permission" symptom; Anthropic clarified general syntax but left the MCP case **explicitly unaddressed**) and **#18837 → #14956** (cluster incl. #13048/#16389: `allowed-tools` "not enforced / ignored entirely"). So **neither Task 169 (`Skill(:*)`) nor Task 171 (specific `mcp__cmk__` names in `permissions.allow`) suppresses these prompts on 2.1.195** — disproven live across cut-gate-v041d→l. **The documented, working mechanism is a `PermissionRequest` hook** (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide#auto-approve-specific-permission-prompts): it fires when CC is about to show a permission dialog and a hook that writes `{"behavior":"allow"}` answers it. **LIVE-PROVEN (cut-gate-v041l):** hooks matching `mcp__cmk__.*` + `Skill` → the popup flashes then auto-dismisses ("Allowed by PermissionRequest hook") and the fact saves with NO click (disk: `feedback_run-black-before-committing.md`; audit `action:created, writeSource:user-explicit`). **Two gates, two fixes:** the per-TOOL/skill gate → the PermissionRequest hook; the SERVER-approval gate → `enabledMcpjsonServers:["cmk"]` (narrow, NOT `enableAllProjectMcpServers`). **The automatic Stop-hook capture path was already prompt-free** (in-process `writeFact`, headless `claude --print`, no MCP/skill) — this fixes the agentic/explicit path. **Shipped (Task 172, additive):** new `cmk-approve-permission` bin + `approve-permission.mjs` (`evaluatePermissionRequest` — approves ONLY kit tools/skills, two-layer safety: narrow matcher + handler self-check, fail-silent); `writeKitHooks` wires the `PermissionRequest` hooks + `enabledMcpjsonServers:["cmk"]` (one write-site, npm + plugin + `repair --hooks`); **KEEP all existing layers** (the full `permissions.allow` + skill `allowed-tools`) as belt-and-suspenders that activate natively when CC fixes #17499/#14956 (the user's call — "maybe everything that worked till now and the rest we added will work someday"). Tests: decision contract (Door 1) + bin stdin→stdout (Door 3) + `enabledMcpjsonServers` idempotency + the wiring (Door 2). Suite 2375/0. **This SUPERSEDES D-211/D-212's allow-list conclusion** as the working fix; Task 169/171 stay as future-proof layers. _Memory: P-S7ML4UPH / P-MYNFF2PW / P-BQ57723C / P-GSNNFRNE / P-4HQTBYFK / P-4ZNAMQM9 / P-6HARUCU5. Relates Task 172 (this fix), Task 108b (`.mcp.json` + `mcp__cmk__*`), Task 169/171 (the kept layers), D-211/D-212 (the disproven conclusion), #17499/#18837/#14956 (external CC-side evidence), the read-what-the-tool-wrote technique._

- **🔬 OPEN INVESTIGATION + PARTIAL-CORRECTION of D-211 (2026-06-27) — D-212: Task 171's allow-list fix does NOT make capture prompt-free; the per-tool `mcp__cmk__<tool>` prompt fires in EVERY folder including this dev repo, and NO kit-writable setting found so far suppresses it. Systematic research pending (post-restart).** This **revises SETTLED D-211 with new live evidence** (per the decision-log re-open rule). D-211 concluded "fix = allow-list each specific tool (Task 171)" — that conclusion is now **DISPROVEN by live test**. Findings this session (2026-06-27), all observed, not theorized:
  - **The prompt fires for every cmk MCP tool, in every folder — including this dev repo `c:/Projects/claude-memory-kit`.** Corrected a FALSE premise that drove the earlier diagnosis: the dev repo is NOT a prompt-free reference. The user: *"I had the same problem as the test project and I needed to allow it a couple of times now in THIS project."* (`mk_remember` "working" this session = the user clicking allow each time, not auto-approval.) It even fired again mid-conversation while we discussed it.
  - **Task 171 (the 11 specific `mcp__cmk__<tool>` names in `permissions.allow`) does NOT suppress the prompt.** A fresh `cut-gate-v041f` install with all 11 names + the wildcard in `settings.json` still prompted on a direct `mk_search` / `mk_remember` call.
  - **`enabledMcpjsonServers: ["cmk"]` in the committed `settings.json` did NOT suppress it.** (Anthropic docs say this field pre-approves project-scope `.mcp.json` servers — observed behavior diverged.)
  - **The dev repo has `enabledMcpjsonServers:["cmk"]` in `settings.local.json` AND `hasTrustDialogAccepted:true` in `~/.claude.json` AND the allow-list — and STILL prompts.** So none of: the allow-list, `enabledMcpjsonServers` (either file), or workspace-trust acceptance pre-approve project-scope `.mcp.json` tools on this CC version.
  - **VERIFIED from code:** the kit registers cmk ONLY at project scope via `.mcp.json` (`settings-hooks.mjs:283-300` `writeKitMcpServer`); there is NO user-scope (`claude mcp add --scope user`) registration path anywhere in the kit.
  - **VERIFIED from Anthropic docs (`code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp`):** *"For security reasons, Claude Code prompts for approval before using project-scoped servers from `.mcp.json` files"* (line 350); project-scope servers awaiting approval show `⏸ Pending approval` (line 163); user-scope servers (`~/.claude.json`, `--scope user`) are NOT subject to the per-project gate (scope table, line 291). The CANDIDATE fix (UNVERIFIED, do NOT ship until tested): register cmk at `--scope user`.
  - **PROCESS — the user's correction (binding, captured `P-ZQ4CCJR7`):** *"all you did till now was running around like a headless chicken. lets do this systematically."* Multiple unverified fix theories were proposed in rapid succession (allow-list → `enabledMcpjsonServers` in settings.json → in settings.local.json → user-scope), several built on the false "dev repo is prompt-free" premise. **Agreed next-step plan: the user restarts the machine; then re-read CC docs (permissions / settings / MCP / CHANGELOG) END-TO-END, build a clean evidence table (what gate fires, per-tool vs per-server, per-session vs every-call, which file CC actually reads MCP approvals from), form ONE hypothesis, TEST it on a FRESH folder (NOT the hand-contaminated v041f), THEN decide.** No fix shipped; nothing tagged; fully reversible.
  - **Don't re-chase the disproven paths.** allow-list (Task 171) ✗ · `enabledMcpjsonServers` in committed settings.json ✗ · same in settings.local.json (untested-but-dev-repo-has-it-and-still-prompts, so ✗-likely) · workspace-trust acceptance alone ✗. _Memory: `P-YQX7SDVV` (corrected facts), `P-ZQ4CCJR7` (process). Relates D-211 (the disproven conclusion this revises), D-209 (Task 169 Skill sibling), Task 108b (the original project-scope `.mcp.json` registration), Task 171 (the allow-list change — now understood as harmless-but-not-the-fix), the live-test-every-task rule._
- **🐛 CUT-GATE FIND + FIX (2026-06-26) — D-211: the real prompt-free break is the MCP-tool gate, not the Skill gate — `mcp__cmk__*` stopped auto-approving per-tool on Claude Code 2.1.x; fix = allow-list each specific tool (Task 171).** _[PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED 2026-06-27 by D-212 above — the "fix = allow-list each specific tool" conclusion was DISPROVEN by live test; Task 171 does not suppress the prompt. The diagnosis of WHICH gate fires (MCP-tool, not Skill) still holds; the FIX did not. See D-212.]_ The v0.4.1 live gate kept prompting on capture even after Task 169 (the Skill `:*` form). The DECISIVE test — strip all Skill entries from a fresh `settings.json`, trigger capture, click "allow (shared)", and READ WHAT CC WRITES — settled it: **(1) the Skill gate is FINE** — CC wrote `Skill(memory-write)` + `Skill(memory-write:*)` to the COMMITTED `settings.json` (so settings.json IS honored for skills — the earlier "only settings.local.json works" theory was WRONG — and the kit's forms were already correct). **(2) the MCP-tool gate is the bug** — a DIRECT `mk_remember` call (outside the skill's `allowed-tools`) tripped *"proceed with mcp__cmk__mk_remember?"* even though `mcp__cmk__*` was in the allow-list, and on "allow" CC wrote the SPECIFIC `mcp__cmk__mk_remember`, NOT the wildcard. So `mcp__cmk__*` no longer auto-approves individual MCP tools on 2.1.x. **The user's "it worked until now" is correct + changelog-backed:** CC 2.1.x churned permission matching (2.1.145 closed a "permission-prompt BYPASS where … were auto-approved"; 2.1.176/2.1.186 wildcard + skill permission fixes) — the wildcard hole was tightened. NOT a kit regression; upstream drift the kit tracks (like the Skill form + Kiro hooks). **Fix:** KIT_ALLOW adds each specific `mcp__cmk__<tool>` (the canonical 11 reused from the Kiro `MCP_AUTO_APPROVE`) + keeps the wildcard. **Why two cut-gate finds in a row (D-209 Skill, D-211 MCP):** both are "the command's report/old form ≠ current reality", both invisible to unit tests (no test drives a live CC approval), both caught only by the live gate + the read-what-CC-writes technique. _Relates Task 108b (the original mcp__cmk__* allow), Task 171 (this fix), D-209 (Task 169, the Skill sibling — now understood as a non-bug: forms were right, this MCP gate was the real cause), the live-test-every-task rule._
- **🐛 CUT-GATE FIND + FIX (2026-06-26) — D-210: `cmk install --with-semantic` verified npm's EXIT CODE instead of the embedder's actual import → falsely disabled semantic (the D-199 class; Task 170).** The v0.4.1 cut-gate (fresh `cut-gate-v041b` install) printed *"semantic recall NOT enabled"* — but the embedder was installed, importable, and `cmk search --mode semantic` returned results. **Root cause (install.mjs:579):** `enableSemantic` bailed on `npm.status !== 0`; on Windows that non-zero exit was a **benign cleanup-EBUSY** (npm couldn't unlink a leftover temp DLL — `sharp-win32-x64`/`libvips-42.dll` held by a running process) AFTER a SUCCESSFUL install. So the kit conflated npm's noisy exit with an install failure and skipped `default_mode: hybrid` — leaving the user silently keyword-only despite doing everything right. **The user's two catches:** *"are you sure semantic is not working?"* (it was — pushed past my premature "it failed") and *"the install needs to be automatic, users are not supposed to do anything"* (the manual `cmk config set` I used to unblock the gate is a workaround, not the fix). **Fix:** on a non-zero npm exit, PROBE the embedder import (`checkEmbedderBinding`, the HC-8 probe); if it imports, enable hybrid regardless of npm's exit — only `nonzero-exit AND import-fails` is a real failure. `enableSemantic` gains an injectable `probeEmbedder` seam. **This is the SECOND D-199-class find this gate** (the first was Task 169's prompt — both "the command's report ≠ reality"), and both were caught only by the LIVE gate (no unit test drives a real npm-with-locked-DLL or a real CC skill-approval). _Relates D-199 (the kiro-cli verify-the-thing lesson this repeats), Task 46 (the original --with-semantic), HC-8 (the embedder probe reused), the live-test-every-task rule._
- **🐛 CUT-GATE FIND + FIX (2026-06-26) — D-209: the `Skill(memory-write)` allow-list stopped suppressing the capture prompt on Claude Code 2.1.x; the fix is to also write the `Skill(<name>:*)` wildcard form (Task 169).** The v0.4.1 live cut-gate (Session 1) hit a "Use skill /memory-write?" approval prompt when the user stated a `.venv` preference — the kit's whole promise is PROMPT-FREE capture (Task 90 allow-lists `Skill(memory-write)` for exactly this). The user: *"the user is never supposed to get this, it's supposed to be always permitted as part of the kit install."* **Diagnosed (primary-source, not guessed):** (1) the kit's `.claude/settings.json` IS correct + the file IS still honored on 2.1.x — permissions MERGE across `settings.json` + `settings.local.json` (verified via claude-code-guide vs code.claude.com/docs/en/settings); (2) **ground truth:** when the user clicked "allow for this project", **Claude Code itself wrote BOTH `Skill(memory-write)` AND `Skill(memory-write:*)`** into `settings.local.json` — so the bare form alone is insufficient on 2.1.x and the `:*` wildcard is now required; (3) the Claude Code docs have a CONFIRMED GAP — `Skill()` permission syntax is undocumented (only Bash/Read/Edit/WebFetch/Agent/MCP are), so CC's own write-behavior is the spec. **NOT a v0.4.1 regression** — the bare form is byte-unchanged since Task 90 and passed every prior gate; **Claude Code changed the matcher**, and the kit tracks it the same way it tracks Kiro hook-format changes (D-198/D-203d). **Fix:** `settings-hooks.mjs` KIT_ALLOW emits BOTH forms for both skills; `validate-skill-allowlist.mjs` normalizes the `:*` suffix to the base skill (parity stays a base-skill check); `cli-install-hooks.test.js` asserts both forms. One write-site covers npm + plugin (both call `writeKitHooks`). **The cut-gate did its job** — a real prompt-free break that unit tests structurally couldn't see (no test drives a live Claude Code skill-approval) was caught by the live gate, exactly per the live-test-every-task rule. _Relates Task 90 (the original allow-list), Task 169 (this fix), the Kiro upstream-format-tracking precedent, the v0.4.1 cut-gate._
- **🔬 LIVE-TEST REVISION (2026-06-26) — D-208: the SessionStart SYNCHRONOUS drain (Q4) is INFEASIBLE — reverted; the detached roll + the cron-liveness gate (167.A) is the real fix.** During the Task 167 autopilot build, the `npm run live-verify:now-roll` agent-loop (real `claude --print`, the automatic path, NO manual command) FAILED: the sync drain fired + detected stale-now correctly, but a **real now→today Haiku roll takes 18–37 s** while the **SessionStart hook ceiling is 30 s**, so it reliably TIMED OUT and fell back to the detached path anyway (live `lazy-compress.log`: `sync-drain timed-out` → detached `compress-session success`). **Re-reading the research closed it:** claude-mem/mem0/Letta compact synchronously at session **END** (the Stop hook, no user waiting), NOT session start — and the kit already has that (the SessionEnd `compress-session` hook). So Q4's PRINCIPLE (correctness > speed) holds, but its MECHANISM (sync-at-start) was wrong — overturned by the live test (the primary source) + a closer research read. **The fix:** keep the now→today roll on the DETACHED SessionStart path + the SessionEnd hook; 167.A (cron-liveness) makes that detached roll reliable (a dead cron can't suppress it) → now.md heals next session and NEVER compounds. `runSyncDrainIfNeeded` + the inject-bin wiring were reverted; 167.B's (a) `now-too-big` verdict proved redundant (stale-now ⊇ it) and (c) sync-catch-up was the reverted code — 167.B ships as "covered by 167.A + 167.F." **Re-verified GREEN** by the live scenario (both single + multi-session: a bloated now.md + dead-cron heartbeat heals from one SessionStart fire). _This is the live-test-every-task rule + the lazy-framing rule applied to our OWN grilled decision: a green unit suite + a confident grilling still shipped an infeasible mechanism; only the real `claude --print` run caught it. Relates D-207 (Q4 revised), the cron-liveness research note, Task 105 (the detached roll it relies on)._
- **🧭 DESIGN RESOLVED (2026-06-25) — D-207: Task 167 = the `compaction-state.mjs` deep module (also architecture-review candidate #1), settled via a 7-question grilling + the marker-vs-derive research + the EverOS/OpenWolf peer-CODE sweep.** **(Q1)** a deep module absorbs the whole verdict — owns `isCompactionNeeded` (replaces `detectStaleness`; the `cron-registered` sentinel + `cronSentinelPath` retire). **(Q2)** marker-vs-derive = HYBRID (the GNU make "Empty Target Files" rule): DERIVE `now`/`daily`/`weekly` from existing artifacts (NO new marker — ADR-0002, the derive logic was never the broken part); STAMP cron-liveness with ONE anacron-style `cron-heartbeat` the cron bins write per fire, gated on AGE not existence. _Peer-validated: OpenWolf's `last_heartbeat`-by-age + EverOS's in-process APScheduler — both schedule reliably ONLY because they have an always-on process the kit lacks → the kit must derive + stamp-per-fire._ **(Q3)** interface = TWO methods — `isCompactionNeeded()` returns a RICH `{verdict, cronStale, heartbeatAge}` (lazy roll reads `.verdict`; doctor/dev reads the rest) + `recordCronHeartbeat()` (single writer). NO standalone `isCronAlive` — one source of truth, can't drift (a 3rd predicate = the exact "two sources disagree" class 167 fixes). **(Q4)** **"we're in the memory business" — correctness > startup speed (binding, P-9V3K7KEA):** stale content + dead cron → drain SYNCHRONOUSLY before inject (THIS session reads clean, not the old heal-next-session); a TIME BUDGET caps the sync portion + finishes detached so it never hangs (offline/huge-file). **(Q5)** cooldown touched on SUCCESS only (failed call must retry); the sync-drain BYPASSES the cooldown (cost guard yields to correctness; it still gates the opportunistic compress). **(Q6)** testing = agent-run, user does NOTHING — unit tests + a live agent-loop scenario in the existing `live-verify.mjs` (trap state → SessionStart-only → assert healed + multi-session-heals-in-one; FORBID any manual drain call — the masking that shipped the original bug green). _The user is on a Claude SUBSCRIPTION → no per-token $ cost gating live tests (P-SW5R69QS)._ **(Q7)** `cmk doctor` HC-10 KEPT as a DEV/diagnostic nice-to-have (helps US during kit dev; informational, never "run X to fix"), NOT a required part of the heal. Memory: P-YZQD9K4U/P-9V3K7KEA/P-UPJDK2UN/P-6PGKSVT3/P-DDRMSBPC/P-SW5R69QS. _Relates D-206 (the bug), the architecture review (candidate #1), the 2026-06-25 research notes (cron-liveness + EverOS), Task 105/106/35._
- **🐛 ROOT CAUSE + TASK (2026-06-25) — D-206: the stale-injection is the lazy now.md→today roll (Task 105) failing to recover a SEVERELY-bloated `now.md` → filed as Task 167, laned v0.4.x, bundled with the 105 lineage (the user: "we need a task for this and lane it for the next version, and do 105 with it").** D-205 found the SYMPTOM (stale "pending" line injected); this is the diagnosed CAUSE. **Task 105 IS shipped + works as designed** (lazy-roll on SessionStart, PR #127 + race-fix #128) — but built/tested against a ~10 KB `now.md`. **The dogfood broke it at scale:** across the v0.4.0 gate sessions (06-20→06-25, many VS Code restarts) `now.md` grew to **410 KB** and the latest `today-*.md` froze at **06-20** — so `inject-context.latestDaySession()` served a 5-day-old day-file. The user's sharp question — *"I restarted VS Code and started a new session, how can it not roll?"* — is exactly right: 105's lazy-on-SessionStart roll SHOULD have fired. **Why it didn't — PROVEN from `lazy-compress.log`, which CORRECTED my first guess (I wrote "times out on 410 KB"; the log says it NEVER RAN — skipped every SessionStart):** two compounding gates, NEITHER size-related. **(1) `cron-active` short-circuit (PRIMARY).** `detectStaleness` (lazy-compress.mjs L216-219) *"Cron sentinel short-circuits everything"* — if `context/.locks/cron-registered` exists → `cron-active` no-op, BEFORE the `stale-now` bloat check (precedence `cron > … > now`). `cmk register-crons` wrote the sentinel → lazy roll defers to "the cron" → **but the host cron NEVER FIRED** (live-confirmed: `Get-ScheduledTask cmk-daily-distill` State "Ready", `LastRunTime` BLANK — machine asleep at the 23:00 trigger). The sentinel disabled the only working path while pointing at a dead primary. **(2) `cooldown` (06-23 on).** The shared 120 s cooldown kept skipping the roll; journal-syncs touch the same marker, holding it fresh. **So `now.md` grew because the roll was GATED OUT, not because it failed** (forced `cmk-compress-session` drained 410 KB→1386 b in 26 s — the roll WORKS; it was never allowed to run). **This PROVES the user's "105 wasn't a real fix, just postponed the problem"** — 105's lazy roll turns itself OFF the instant a cron sentinel exists, with no check the cron is alive. **SCOPE (the user's catch: "the cron isn't registered by default, right?" — correct):** `cmk install` does NOT register crons (`register-crons` is OPTIONAL — README: "otherwise self-heals lazily"). So the **default user is NOT affected** (no sentinel → lazy roll works). This bites ONLY a `register-crons` user whose cron doesn't actually fire — and the nasty part is that **opting into the cron makes it WORSE than not** (the sentinel disables the working lazy fallback, then points at a dead primary). Medium severity, but a trap on the careful-user path. _Sibling to D-179 (2026-06-20, same week): the daily-distill itself went 4 days stale via a cron/timeout issue — the same "trust-the-cron / if-it's-not-automatic-it's-a-bug" class, caught by the same user instinct._ **Fix (Task 167, split into 3 sub-bugs, TDD, 105 lineage):** 167.A cron-LIVENESS gate (short-circuit only if the cron actually ran recently, not sentinel-exists — the highest-leverage one-line cause); 167.B size-triggered `now-too-big` verdict outranking `cron-active` + cooldown carve-out + chunked/sync catch-up; 167.C observability WARN + a `cmk doctor` HC-10 for bloated-now.md / stale-today / dead-cron. **Immediate data fix done this session** (ran `cmk-compress-session` by hand → fresh `today-2026-06-25.md`; inject verified clean: `RESEARCH-FIRST` count 0). **RE-LANED (the user, 2026-06-25): Task 167 = the NEXT version (v0.4.1); Task 151 persona = the version after; Cursor (was v0.4.1) postponed further out.** _Relates D-205 (the symptom), Task 105 (the roll it extends) / 106 (the rename-race a sync catch-up must preserve) / 35 (the cron path), `lazy-compress.mjs` `detectStaleness`, `inject-context.mjs` `latestDaySession`, `compress-session.mjs`, §6 rolling-window._
- **🐛 KIT-DOGFOOD BUG (2026-06-25) — D-205: the rolling session summary (`recent.md`) does NOT retire RESOLVED threads → a shipped epic leaves a stale "pending" line in the injected snapshot (the kit's own cross-session-amnesia, on itself).** A fresh session after the v0.4.0 ship read the injected snapshot and concluded "next work = the v0.4.0 Kiro adapter seam, RESEARCH-FIRST, pending — use RESUME-HERE.md" — but v0.4.0 had ALREADY SHIPPED (npm confirms `0.4.0`). The user caught it: *"why is it stale? this is the whole point of this kit."* **Root cause (diagnosed, not guessed):** TWO injected tiers behaved differently. (1) **MEMORY.md auto-capture WORKED** — its Active Threads are current (06-25 timestamps: IDE-1.0.52-download, chose-to-upgrade, clean-test-folders). (2) **`context/sessions/recent.md` (the rolling-window summary) is FROZEN at 06-20** — its "Active Threads" still lists *"v0.4.0 Kiro adapter: RESEARCH-FIRST against kiro.dev §5.1 (use RESUME-HERE.md)"*, written BEFORE the campaign started. The rolling compression carries old summaries forward VERBATIM; **nothing reconciles "this thread RESOLVED" against the summary tier.** So a *completed* multi-day epic injects a "pending" ghost. **The gap (the real lesson):** the kit captures NEW facts well; it does NOT propagate thread-RESOLUTION to the rolling summary. **Fix (this session):** corrected `recent.md`'s Active Threads (✅ RESOLVED — v0.4.0 SHIPPED) + captured the finding (`P-VaK5DNNa`). **v0.x candidate:** a "thread-resolution sweep" in `compress-session` — when a thread resolves (epic ships / task closes), drop or mark-RESOLVED the matching Active-Threads line in `recent.md` rather than rolling it forward. Until automated, a session that ships a major lane should correct `recent.md`'s Active Threads. _Relates the whole-kit thesis (kill cross-session amnesia — this is it, applied to the kit's OWN summaries), D-203n (the ship this stale line contradicted), the rolling-window compression (§6) which has no resolution-reconciliation step._

All three surfaces live-proven (Claude Code, Kiro IDE 1.0.52, kiro-cli V3 2.9.0). **DOC SWEEP (the user: "did we update README/MCP/CLI/architecture?"):** ✅ DONE — main README (cross-agent Features bullet + permissions surface in the Kiro table + uninstall), `packages/cli/README.md` (npm landing: works-across-agents bullet, stale `~/.aws`→`~/.kiro` D-198 fix, execute_bash→PreToolUse), `docs/CLI.md` (--ide kiro + uninstall: v1 hooks + 5 CLI-agent hooks + permissions.yaml + stale `~/.aws` fix). ✅ `validate-doc-completeness` green (all 33 CLI verbs + 11 MCP tools documented — CLI.md/MCP.md structurally complete; MCP.md needs no Kiro content — tools are agent-agnostic). **⏳ REMAINING DOC items (low-priority, NOT user-facing-critical — the published READMEs + CLI.md are correct):** (a) `specs/design.md` — has 15 Kiro mentions (the cross-agent seam) but may not document the IDE-1.0 v1-hook format / messages.jsonl adapter / permissions.yaml — spot-check + add a §; (b) `HEALTH-CHECKS.md` — 0 Kiro mentions; the HC-1 agent-awareness (D-185) isn't reflected; (c) `RESUME-HERE.md` lines 37/40/45/54 — historical `~/.aws`/`MEMORY_KIT_AWS_DIR` refs (handoff doc, partly point-in-time — lower priority). **NEXT-SESSION PICKUP:** v0.4.0 is shipped + the user-facing docs are correct; finish the 3 internal-doc items above (design.md §, HEALTH-CHECKS Kiro note, RESUME-HERE cleanup) when convenient. Full arc: DECISION-LOG D-185→D-203n. _Relates the every-PR doc rule (the per-PR README edits were incomplete — this sweep finished them post-ship), D-198 (the stale-aws class)._
- **📍 (superseded by D-203n above) SESSION CHECKPOINT (2026-06-25) — D-203m: v0.4.0 Kiro work is CODE-COMPLETE + live-proven on ALL THREE surfaces; only the TAG remains.** **Done this session (all merged to main):** Task 50.N parity campaign — #228 prompt-capture, #229 observe-edit (CLI), #230 IDE v1 hooks, #231 v1 filename fix, #232 IDE-1.0 transcript adapter (messages.jsonl), #233 permissions.yaml pre-trust; Task 50.O (kiro-cli D-197→200). **LIVE-PROVEN (D-203e→k):** Kiro IDE 1.0.52 — auto-loads v1 hooks, dual-emit no-double-fire, full memory loop (inject/capture/observe/mk_remember/wedge) PROMPT-FREE incl. skill-load (50.N.5 permissions.yaml). kiro-cli V3 2.9.0 — hooks fire, `cmk remember` prompt-free (agent-config trust), wedge promotes. **SETTLED:** the two Kiro surfaces use DIFFERENT trust — IDE = per-workspace `~/.kiro/workspace-roots/<hash>/permissions.yaml` (hash=sha256(norm(projectRoot)).slice(0,16)); kiro-cli V3 = agent-config `toolsSettings.shell.allowedCommands`. The kit writes BOTH. **SUITE 2320+/0.** **USER-TIER NOTE:** the user's real persona was never a single backup (all gate-test sessions, never carried forward — the user correctly realized this); reset to a fresh seeded tier (`cmk init-user-tier`); gate-noise archived in `C:\cut-gate-backups\`. Genuine memory lives in this repo's `context/` + git (intact). **REMAINING before the v0.4.0 tag (the user's outward steps):** (1) the user pushes the `v0.4.0` tag → publish.yml (npm + GitHub Release); (2) optional: finish E1/KU1/KU2 uninstall gate items. **KNOWN DEFER:** kiro-cli V3 delete-guardrail (`preToolUse` doesn't fire on V3) → Task 166 (not a blocker; Kiro's native shell-approval covers it). **NEXT-SESSION PICKUP:** the kit fully works on Claude Code + Kiro IDE 1.0 + kiro-cli V3; tag v0.4.0 when ready. _Relates D-203a→l (the full IDE-1.0 discovery arc), Task 50/50.N/50.O._
- **🐛 BUG + 🔧 FIX (2026-06-22) — D-195: the `memory-write` SKILL.md `description` was INVALID YAML (an unquoted `: ` colon-space) — Claude Code tolerated it, Kiro REJECTED the skill ("Invalid SKILL.md frontmatter"); fixed with a YAML block scalar + a strict-parse validator (the 7th live-found cross-agent cut-blocker).** The cut-gate-kiro live test surfaced it the moment Kiro loaded the project: a red "Invalid SKILL.md frontmatter: …/.kiro/skills/memory-write/SKILL.md". **Root cause:** the `description:` was a plain (unquoted) YAML scalar containing the phrase `"update memory: X is now Y"` — and in YAML a `: ` (colon-space) inside a plain scalar starts a new mapping key, so a strict parser throws `mapping values are not allowed here` (line 3, col 607) and rejects the whole frontmatter. **Independently confirmed by two strict parsers:** my js-yaml AND Kiro's own PyYAML (the user asked Kiro why, and its `yaml.safe_load` threw the identical error at the identical column). **The Claude-tolerated/Kiro-strict class (same theme as D-185..190/194 — the 7th):** Claude Code reads SKILL.md frontmatter LENIENTLY (cares about name/description as text, doesn't strict-parse the block), so the invalid YAML shipped LATENT and "worked" for months; Kiro strict-parses the frontmatter for progressive skill-loading and rejects it. `memory-search` happened to parse (no `: ` in its description) — fragile by luck, not design. **The fix (the more robust one — Kiro itself suggested it over my first em-dash rephrase):** convert BOTH skills' `description` to a YAML **folded block scalar** (`description: >-`), which treats `:`, quotes, and apostrophes as literal text — immune to the whole class, not just this one phrase (the description is full of `"…"` and `don't`, so neither double- nor single-quoting works; the block scalar is the clean fix). **Single-source discipline (the user's "aren't we working with a template?"):** fixed ONLY the canonical `template/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, then `sync-plugin-skills.mjs` for the plugin copy + re-copied to the dogfood `.claude/skills/` — one source, both agents inherit it (Claude Code scaffolds it verbatim; Kiro via `kiro-skills.mjs` translating `context:`/`allowed-tools:` away — composition VERIFIED: the translated output parses + drops the right keys). **The structural guard (so it can't recur):** `validate-skill-sources.mjs` had its OWN naive line-split frontmatter parser (as lenient as Claude Code's — which is why the validator never caught it); replaced with a **strict js-yaml parse** that fails the gate naming the YAML reason. PLUS the integration guard: `cli-kiro-skills.test.js` now strict-parses the TRANSLATED `.kiro/skills/` output (what Kiro actually consumes) — the test that would have caught it originally. Also migrated `cli-skill-scaffold.test.js`'s naive frontmatter parser to js-yaml (it returned an empty description on the block scalar). **Both agents, one fix:** the canonical block-scalar fix + the strict-parse validator protect Claude Code AND Kiro scaffolding at once. Test-first: `scripts-validate-skill-sources.test.js` (real-skills-pass / colon-space-fails / colon-without-space-ok) + the kiro-skills integration test.
